30:0.rendering: The words of King Lemuel, the oracle his mother taught him.
30:0.note: This rare glimpse into a queen mother's instruction to her royal son echoes the ancient Near Eastern tradition of maternal wisdom, setting the stage for the famous poem that follows.

30:1.rendering: What are you doing, my son? What are you doing, son of my womb? What are you doing, son of my vows?
30:1.note: The triple "what" (mah in Hebrew) echoes a mother's urgent, rhythmic cry, like a lament or wake-up call to her royal son.

30:2.rendering: Do not give your strength to women, or your ways to that which destroys kings.
30:2.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" (chayil) is the same one used for the capable wife later in this chapter, creating a deliberate contrast between squandering royal vigor on fleeting pleasures and stewarding it wisely.

30:3.rendering: It is not for kings, Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, or for rulers to crave strong drink,
30:3.note: The double "not for kings" echoes ancient royal wisdom warnings, guarding the ruler's clear mind for justice.

30:4.rendering: lest they drink and forget what is decreed, and pervert the rights of all the afflicted.
30:4.note: The word for "decreed" here echoes the "ordinance" given to kings in Deuteronomy 17:18-20, warning against royal excess that distorts justice.

30:5.rendering: Give strong drink to the one who is perishing, and wine to those who are bitter in soul.
30:5.note: This proverb echoes the ancient custom of offering a sedative drink to ease the suffering of the dying or deeply distressed, a practice later echoed at the cross in Matthew 27.

30:6.rendering: Let them drink and forget their poverty, and remember their misery no more.
30:6.note: This verse echoes the ancient royal wisdom of Proverbs 31:4-5, where kings are warned against strong drink, but here it offers merciful relief to the destitute.

30:7.rendering: Open your mouth for the voiceless, for the justice of all who are passing away.
30:7.note: The Hebrew word for "voiceless" (literally "mute") echoes the cries of the oppressed in Exodus, urging the powerful to speak where the vulnerable cannot.

30:8.rendering: Speak up and judge fairly, defend the rights of the poor and needy.
30:8.note: The Hebrew verb for "speak up" echoes the same urgent call used for advocates in the city gate, where justice was decided in ancient Israel.

30:9.rendering: A wife of noble character, who can find? Her worth is far beyond jewels.
30:9.note: The Hebrew phrase "eshet chayil" echoes the "mighty man of valor" from battle stories, reframing strength as household wisdom and industry.

30:10.rendering: The heart of her husband trusts in her, and he has no lack of gain.
30:10.note: The word "gain" (shalal) echoes the plunder of battle, portraying this wife as a strategic partner who brings home victory rather than vulnerability.

30:11.rendering: She brings him good, and not harm, all the days of her life.
30:11.note: The Hebrew word for "good" here echoes the creation refrain "it was good," painting this wife as a partner who keeps echoing God's own life-giving work in the home.

30:12.rendering: She seeks out wool and flax, and works with willing hands.
30:12.note: The Hebrew verb for "seeks" (d-r-sh) is the same one used for diligently searching out wisdom or the Lord's will elsewhere in Proverbs.

30:13.rendering: She is like the ships of the merchant, she brings her food from afar.
30:13.note: The Hebrew word for "food" here is the same one used for the manna God rained from heaven, portraying this woman as a provider who reaches across distances like divine supply in the wilderness.

30:14.rendering: She rises while it is still night, and gives food to her household, and a portion to her servant girls.
30:14.note: The Hebrew word for "portion" (choq) echoes the manna given in the wilderness, portraying this woman as a provider who sustains her community with both daily bread and justice.

30:15.rendering: She considers a field and buys it, from the fruit of her hands she plants a vineyard.
30:15.note: The Hebrew word for "considers" carries the sense of plotting or planning with shrewd wisdom, echoing the same verb used for the serpent in Eden.

30:16.rendering: She wraps her loins with strength, and she makes her arms strong.
30:16.note: The Hebrew verb for "makes strong" echoes God's own strengthening of his people in battle, portraying this woman's vigor as a divine-like fortitude.

30:17.rendering: She senses that her trading is good, and her lamp does not go out at night.
30:17.note: The Hebrew word for "senses" literally means "tastes," echoing how Wisdom is a tree of life whose fruit is sweet (Prov 3:18).

30:18.rendering: She stretches out her hands to the distaff, and her palms grasp the spindle.
30:18.note: The Hebrew word for "stretches out" echoes the capable woman's eager hands in verse 13, painting her as one who actively embraces the ancient tools of provision rather than merely enduring them.

30:19.rendering: She opens her palm to the poor, and she stretches out her hands to the needy.
30:19.note: The Hebrew word for "hands" here echoes the same term used for the capable wife's own strong hands in verse 19, linking her personal strength directly to generosity.

30:20.rendering: She is not afraid of the snow for her household, for all her household is clothed in scarlet.
30:20.note: The Hebrew word for "scarlet" (shanim) echoes the bright red coverings of the tabernacle, hinting that her provision creates a kind of sacred shelter for her people.

30:21.rendering: She makes coverings for herself from fine linen and purple cloth. Her clothing is high quality and beautiful.
30:21.note: The Hebrew word for "coverings" echoes the tabernacle fabrics in Exodus, hinting that this woman's household becomes a kind of sacred space through her skillful hands.

30:22.rendering: Her husband is respected at the city gate, where he sits among the elders of the land.
30:22.note: In the ancient Near East the city gate was the town square for business and justice, so this single line paints the husband as a man of public honor whose dignity is quietly built on his wife's industry.

30:23.rendering: She makes linen garments and sells them, and supplies sashes to the merchants of Canaan.
30:23.note: The Hebrew word for "merchants" literally means "Canaanites," an ancient echo of Israel's old rivals now reframed as honest trading partners in this portrait of industry.

30:24.rendering: She is clothed with strength and dignity, and she laughs at the days to come.
30:24.note: The Hebrew word for "laughs" echoes Sarah's astonished laughter in Genesis 18, turning future anxiety into confident joy.

30:25.rendering: She opens her mouth with wisdom, and the teaching of kindness is on her tongue.
30:25.note: The Hebrew pairs "wisdom" (hokmah) with "teaching of kindness" (torat chesed), echoing how the whole book of Proverbs ends with a portrait of lived-out wisdom that feels like torah wrapped in mercy.

30:26.rendering: She watches over the goings on of her household, and the bread of idleness she does not eat.
30:26.note: The Hebrew word for "watches over" is the same one used for a soldier standing guard on the city walls, turning domestic life into a vigilant vocation.

30:27.rendering: Her children rise up and call her happy, her husband also, and he praises her.
30:27.note: The Hebrew verb for "rise up" echoes the same word used for the capable woman "rising" while it is still night in verse 15, linking her diligence to the honor she receives.

30:28.rendering: Many daughters have done nobly, but you surpass them all.
30:28.note: The Hebrew word for "nobly" (chayil) is the same one used for the capable warrior in Proverbs 31:10, quietly celebrating this woman's strength in an ancient world that rarely praised it.

30:29.rendering: Charm is deceptive, and beauty is vapour, but a woman who fears the Lord, she shall be praised.
30:29.note: The word "vapour" (hebel) echoes the preacher's cry in Ecclesiastes, showing even the best of life is fleeting except for the fear of the Lord.

30:30.rendering: Give her the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her in the city gates.
30:30.note: The Hebrew word for "works" echoes the same term used for God's creative acts in Genesis, portraying this woman's labor as a reflection of divine creativity.

29:0.rendering: The words of Agur son of Jakeh, the oracle. The man's declaration: I am weary, God, I am weary and worn out.
29:0.note: Agur's opening Hebrew wordplay on "I am weary" (la'ayit) echoes the sound of his own name, turning his exhaustion into a signature on this raw, wisdom-from-the-margins collection.

29:1.rendering: Surely I am more stupid than any man, and I do not have the understanding of a human being.
29:1.note: The Hebrew word for "stupid" here is ba'ar, the same term used for a brutish beast, revealing the author's raw humility before wisdom's demands.

29:2.rendering: I have not learned wisdom, nor have I come to know the Holy One.
29:2.note: The Hebrew "q'doshim" hints at more than one, echoing the mysterious plurality of the divine council the sage has not yet glimpsed.

29:3.rendering: Who has gone up to heaven and come down? Who has gathered the wind in his fists? Who has wrapped up the waters in a garment? Who has established all the ends of the earth? What is his name, and what is the name of his son? Surely you know!
29:3.note: The ancient riddle piles up images of the Creator's untouchable power, then slips in the question about his son, an early whisper that the divine King has an heir.

29:4.rendering: Every word of God is refined like silver, a shield to those who take refuge in him.
29:4.note: The Hebrew "refined" echoes the smelting imagery of Psalm 12:6 and 18:30, showing God's words as tested and trustworthy.

29:5.rendering: Do not add to his words, or he will rebuke you and you will be found a liar.
29:5.note: This echoes Deuteronomy 4:2 and 12:32, where the command against tampering with God's words carries the same warning of judgment.

29:6.rendering: Two things I ask of you, do not withhold them from me before I die:
29:6.note: Agur's urgent, deathbed plea echoes the ancient Near Eastern pattern of final requests from the wise, framing his prayer as a last chance for divine favor.

29:7.rendering: Keep deception and lies far from me. Give me neither poverty nor riches. Feed me with the food that is my portion.
29:7.note: The Hebrew word for "portion" echoes the manna story in Exodus 16, where God gave each Israelite exactly what they needed for the day.

29:8.rendering: lest I become full and deny you, and say, "Who is the LORD?" Or lest I become poor and steal, and profane the name of my God.
29:8.note: The Hebrew word for "deny" echoes Israel's wilderness grumbling, revealing how both wealth and want can erode our daily dependence on God.

29:9.rendering: Do not slander a servant to his master, or he will curse you and you will bear the guilt.
29:9.note: The Hebrew word for "slander" here echoes the same root used for false accusation in the Ten Commandments, highlighting how damaging words create guilt that sticks to the speaker.

29:10.rendering: There is a generation that curses its father and does not bless its mother.
29:10.note: The Hebrew word for "generation" here echoes the creation language of "toledot," turning family order upside down.

29:11.rendering: There is a generation that is pure in its own eyes, yet it is not washed from its filth.
29:11.note: The Hebrew word for "generation" carries a communal weight, exposing how entire groups can share a collective self-deception about their moral state.

29:12.rendering: There is a generation, how lofty are its eyes, and its eyelids are lifted high.
29:12.note: The Hebrew word for "lifted high" echoes the serpent's arrogant gaze in Genesis, painting a portrait of eyes that refuse to look down in humility.

29:13.rendering: There is a generation whose teeth are swords, whose fangs are knives, to devour the poor from the earth and the needy from among humanity.
29:13.note: The Hebrew word for "generation" here echoes the repeated "dor" in verses 11-14, painting four portraits of a toxic kind of people who prey on the vulnerable.

29:14.rendering: The leech has two daughters: "Give!" and "Give!" Three things are never satisfied, four never say "Enough!"
29:14.note: This numerical proverb uses the ancient pattern of "three, even four" to highlight insatiable greed, with the leech's cry echoing the daughters' endless demand.

29:15.rendering: The grave, a barren womb, land that never has enough water, and fire that never says "Enough."
29:15.note: The original Hebrew repeats "not satisfied" (lo tisba) like a drumbeat, painting four things that devour without end.

29:16.rendering: The eye that mocks a father and scorns the obedience of a mother, the ravens of the valley will pluck it out, and the vultures will eat it.
29:16.note: This vivid curse draws on ancient Near Eastern imagery of scavenger birds as agents of shameful judgment, echoing the dishonor of breaking the fifth commandment.

29:17.rendering: Three things are too wonderful for me, four I do not understand:
29:17.note: The numerical proverb form (three, even four) creates a rhythmic escalation that signals deep wonder rather than a rigid list.

29:18.rendering: the way of an eagle in the sky, the way of a snake on a rock, the way of a ship in the heart of the sea, and the way of a man with a young woman.
29:18.note: The Hebrew word for "way" repeats four times, linking the mystery of soaring flight, slithering stealth, ocean navigation, and human desire as equally wondrous and unknowable.

29:19.rendering: This is the way of an adulterous woman: she eats and wipes her mouth, and says, "I have done no wrong."
29:19.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "eat" and "mouth" echoes the garden's forbidden fruit, exposing how sin disguises itself as everyday innocence.

29:20.rendering: Under three things the earth trembles, under four it cannot bear up:
29:20.note: The numerical saying form here uses a x, x+1 pattern common in wisdom literature to signal emphasis and totality.

29:21.rendering: Under three things the earth trembles, and under four it cannot bear up: a slave when he becomes king, and a fool when he is filled with food.
29:21.note: The Hebrew pairs this with a landlady who gets an inheritance and a maidservant who displaces her mistress, exposing how sudden power overturns social order and invites chaos.

29:22.rendering: Under three things the earth trembles, and under four it cannot bear up: ... an unloved woman when she gets a husband, and a maidservant when she succeeds her mistress.
29:22.note: The Hebrew verb for "trembles" echoes the shaking of the ground under locusts in verse 27, painting social upheaval as a force of nature.

29:23.rendering: Four things on earth are small, yet they are exceedingly wise:
29:23.note: The Hebrew word for "wise" here echoes the same term used for the skilled craftsman Bezalel in Exodus, revealing how wisdom shows up in the overlooked and ordinary.

29:24.rendering: The ants are a people not strong, yet they prepare their food in the summer.
29:24.note: The Hebrew word for "people" is the same one used for Israel, quietly likening these tiny creatures to God's own nation that thrives by wise foresight.

29:25.rendering: the rock badgers are a people not mighty, yet they make their homes in the cliffs.
29:25.note: Rock badgers show wisdom by building in the high rocks, echoing the theme of the prudent finding refuge where the strong cannot.

29:26.rendering: Locusts have no king, yet they all march out in ranks.
29:26.note: The Hebrew word for "ranks" echoes military order, highlighting wisdom in collective discipline without a visible leader.

29:27.rendering: The lizard you can catch with your hands, yet it is in kings' palaces.
29:27.note: The Hebrew word for "lizard" carries a sense of something easily crushed, yet this fragile creature boldly claims space in royal halls, an image of humble tenacity.

29:28.rendering: There are three things that stride with stately step, four that move with dignity:
29:28.note: The numerical ladder of three then four echoes the whole chapter's pattern of ordered observation, inviting us to watch the world with quiet wonder.

29:29.rendering: The lion, which is mightiest among beasts, and does not turn back before any.
29:29.note: The Hebrew word for "lion" here (layish) is a rare poetic term that pictures an undefeated king of the wild, an image echoed in Judah's lion-cub blessing in Genesis 49:9.

1:0.rendering: My son, if you accept my words and store up my commands within you,
1:0.note: The Hebrew pairs "accept" and "store up" like a treasure hunter who both receives and hides valuables, echoing the search for wisdom in the next verses.

1:1.rendering: Make your ear pay close attention to wisdom, and bend your heart toward understanding.
1:1.note: The Hebrew pairs "ear" with active listening and "heart" with intentional inclination, showing wisdom demands both hearing and wholehearted pursuit.

1:2.rendering: Yes, if you cry out for insight, and lift your voice for understanding,
1:2.note: The Hebrew verb for "cry out" echoes a child's urgent call to a parent, inviting us to pursue wisdom with the same desperate intensity.

1:3.rendering: If you seek her like silver, and search for her as for hidden treasures,
1:3.note: The Hebrew word for "search" here is the same one used for digging up buried treasure in the ancient world, turning wisdom into an active quest rather than passive hope.

1:4.rendering: Then you will understand the fear of the Lord, and find the knowledge of God.
1:4.note: This "then" marks the payoff after verses 1-4's urgent hunt for wisdom, echoing the search for hidden treasure.

1:5.rendering: For the Lord gives wisdom, from his mouth come knowledge and understanding.
1:5.note: This verse echoes the creation story, where God's mouth speaks order and life into chaos, now offering that same creative wisdom to those who seek it.

1:6.rendering: He stores up sound wisdom for the upright, he is a shield to those who walk in integrity.
1:6.note: The Hebrew for "sound wisdom" is "tushiyah," a rare word evoking something substantial and effective, like a successful plan or resource that God himself hoards for the upright.

1:7.rendering: He guards the paths of justice, and he watches over the way of his loyal ones.
1:7.note: The word for "loyal ones" (hasidim) echoes God's own loyal-love (hesed), creating a beautiful symmetry of faithfulness.

0:0.rendering: The proverbs of Solomon, son of David, king of Israel.
0:0.note: This opening echoes the ancient Near Eastern tradition of royal wisdom collections, positioning Solomon as a new David who gathers insight for God's people.

0:1.rendering: To know wisdom and instruction, to discern the words of insight.
0:1.note: The Hebrew word for "know" here implies intimate, transformative acquaintance, not just collecting facts.

0:2.rendering: To receive training in wise dealing, righteousness, justice, and equity.
0:2.note: The Hebrew word for "training" here is the same one used for disciplining a child or breaking in a horse, hinting that wisdom is formed through repeated, often uncomfortable practice.

0:3.rendering: To give shrewdness to the naive, knowledge and discretion to the young.
0:3.note: The Hebrew word for shrewdness here is the same one used for the serpent in Genesis 3, turning a dangerous trait into a gift of wisdom for the simple.

0:4.rendering: Let the wise hear and increase in learning, and the understanding one acquire wise guidance.
0:4.note: The Hebrew word for "guidance" here literally means "steering" or "ropes," picturing wisdom as the skill that keeps life on course.

0:5.rendering: to understand a proverb and a riddle, the words of the wise and their riddles.
0:5.note: The Hebrew pairs "mashal" (proverb) with "melitzah" (riddle or enigmatic saying), inviting readers into layered wisdom that unfolds slowly through meditation.

0:6.rendering: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge, but fools despise wisdom and instruction.
0:6.note: This opening line plays on the Hebrew word for "beginning" (reshit), which echoes the very first word of Genesis, signaling that true knowledge starts with awe before the Creator.

0:7.rendering: My son, listen to the instruction of your father, and do not forsake the teaching of your mother.
0:7.note: The Hebrew pairs "father's instruction" with "mother's teaching," showing both parents as authoritative wisdom teachers in ancient Israelite homes.

0:8.rendering: They will be a graceful wreath for your head, and ornaments for your neck.
0:8.note: The Hebrew word for "wreath" echoes the garlands worn by honored guests at ancient feasts, picturing wisdom as public honor that adorns the whole person.

0:9.rendering: My son, if sinners entice you, do not consent.
0:9.note: The Hebrew word for "entice" carries the idea of being seduced or persuaded into a trap, echoing the snake in Eden.

0:10.rendering: If they say, "Come with us, let us lie in wait for blood, let us ambush the innocent without cause."
0:10.note: The Hebrew word for "ambush" echoes the same term used for hunting animals, exposing how the wicked treat people like prey.

0:11.rendering: Let us swallow them alive like Sheol, whole as those who go down to the pit.
0:11.note: The Hebrew wordplay pits the gang's boast against Sheol itself, the greedy grave that swallows the living whole, exposing how violence mimics the very death it deals.

0:12.rendering: We will find all kinds of precious wealth, we will fill our houses with plunder.
0:12.note: The Hebrew word for "plunder" echoes the violent spoils of war, exposing how the sinners' promise twists honest gain into robbery.

0:13.rendering: Throw in your lot with us, and we will all have one common purse.
0:13.note: The Hebrew verb for "throw in your lot" literally means to cast a stone or lot together, echoing the ancient practice of deciding shares by dice or stones.

0:14.rendering: My son, do not walk in the way with them. Keep your foot from their path.
0:14.note: The Hebrew word for "path" here echoes the very first verse of the Psalms, where the righteous avoid the way of sinners.

0:15.rendering: For their feet run to evil, and they hurry to shed blood.
0:15.note: This vivid image of racing feet echoes Isaiah 59:7, painting sin as an addictive sprint rather than a mere stumble.

1:8.rendering: Then you will understand righteousness and justice, and equity, every good path.
1:8.note: The Hebrew word for "understand" here echoes its use in verse 5, linking the fear of the Lord directly to moral discernment in daily life.

1:9.rendering: For wisdom will enter your heart, and knowledge will be pleasant to your soul.
1:9.note: The Hebrew pairs "heart" (mind and will) with "soul" (whole self), showing wisdom reshaping not just what you know but who you are from the inside out.

1:10.rendering: Discretion will watch over you, and understanding will guard you.
1:10.note: The Hebrew word for "watch over" is the same one used for the cherubim guarding Eden, turning wisdom into a fierce protector.

1:11.rendering: To rescue you from the way of evil, from the man who speaks perverse things.
1:11.note: The Hebrew word for "perverse" literally means "twisted," echoing the serpent's crooked path in Genesis 3.

1:12.rendering: Those who abandon the straight paths to walk in dark ways.
1:12.note: The Hebrew word for "paths" echoes the "way" of wisdom in verse 8, exposing how rejecting straight roads leads into shadowed, crooked territory.

1:13.rendering: who rejoice in doing evil, and delight in the twistedness of evil people,
1:13.note: The Hebrew word for "twistedness" (tahpukhot) echoes the same term used for the "crooked" path that wisdom's rivals walk in verse 15, painting evil not as mere bad choices but as a joyful celebration of life's most warped detours.

1:14.rendering: whose paths are crooked, and whose tracks are twisted.
1:14.note: The Hebrew word for "crooked" here is the same one used for the twisting of the serpent in Genesis 3, painting these wrong paths as a return to the ancient, deceptive bend in the garden.

1:15.rendering: It will rescue you from the strange woman, from the foreign woman who makes her words smooth.
1:15.note: The Hebrew word for "foreign" carries the double sense of outsider and adulteress, echoing the warnings against strange fire and strange gods in the Torah.

0:16.rendering: In vain the net is spread in full view of any bird.
0:16.note: This proverb uses the image of a hunter spreading a snare right where the bird can see it, ironically showing how the wicked ignore obvious danger.

0:17.rendering: But these men lie in wait for their own blood, they set an ambush for their own lives.
0:17.note: The Hebrew word for "ambush" (ya'arab) echoes the very same verb used in verse 11 when they plotted against the innocent, exposing the self-destructive trap they are setting.

0:18.rendering: Such are the paths of everyone who is greedy for gain. It takes the life of its owners.
0:18.note: The Hebrew word for "gain" here echoes the violent "booty" seized in raids, showing greed turns its own people into the prey.

0:19.rendering: Wisdom cries aloud in the street, she lifts her voice in the public squares.
0:19.note: The Hebrew verb for "cries aloud" is the same one used for a street vendor shouting her wares, portraying Wisdom as a bold woman calling out in the busiest places.

0:20.rendering: She cries aloud at the head of the noisy streets, at the entrance of the city gates she speaks her words.
0:20.note: The Hebrew verb for her crying out echoes the same root used for a town crier making a public proclamation, showing Lady Wisdom claims the most public spaces of ancient city life.

0:21.rendering: How long, you naive ones, will you love your naivety? How long will scoffers delight in their scoffing, and fools hate knowledge?
0:21.note: The Hebrew word for "naive ones" echoes the "simple" youth in the book's opening, painting three escalating portraits of resistance to wisdom.

0:22.rendering: Turn back at my rebuke. Look, I will pour out my spirit on you, I will make my words known to you.
0:22.note: The Hebrew for "turn back" echoes the prophets' call to repentance, while "pour out my spirit" foreshadows the same promise in Joel 2:28 and Acts 2.

0:23.rendering: Because I called and you refused, I stretched out my hand and no one paid attention,
0:23.note: The Hebrew verb for "stretched out my hand" echoes God's powerful gestures of deliverance in Exodus, now turned to an offer of wisdom that is spurned.

1:16.rendering: She abandons the companion of her youth, and forgets the covenant of her God.
1:16.note: The Hebrew word for "companion" (alluph) carries a double meaning of "intimate friend" and "tame ox," evoking the betrayal of a lifelong partnership.

1:17.rendering: For her house sinks down to death, and her paths lead to the shades of the dead.
1:17.note: The Hebrew word for "sinks" echoes the falling of the foolish in Proverbs 1:32, painting her house as a trapdoor straight into the realm of the dead.

1:18.rendering: None who go to her come back again, nor do they regain the paths of life.
1:18.note: The Hebrew verb for "regain" echoes the wisdom language of "taking hold" of life itself, a sobering picture of irreversible entrapment.

1:19.rendering: So walk in the way of the good, and keep to the paths of the righteous.
1:19.note: This verse echoes the book's opening call to "walk" in wisdom's paths, contrasting the crooked ways of the wicked just described.

1:20.rendering: For the upright will inhabit the land, and the blameless will remain in it.
1:20.note: This echoes the covenant promise of the land from Deuteronomy, using the same Hebrew verb for "remain" that describes Israel's secure inheritance.

1:21.rendering: But the wicked will be cut off from the land, and the treacherous will be torn out of it.
1:21.note: This verse echoes the covenant warning in Deuteronomy 28, where staying rooted in the land depends on covenant faithfulness, not just moral effort.

0:24.rendering: You ignored all my advice, and you would not accept my correction.
0:24.note: The Hebrew word for "correction" (tokhachah) is the same one used for a father's loving discipline in Proverbs 3:11, showing wisdom's words as parental care rejected.

0:25.rendering: So I also will laugh at your disaster, I will mock when dread comes upon you.
0:25.note: The Hebrew word for "laugh" here is the same one used for God's laughter at the rebellious kings in Psalm 2:4, an intertextual echo that portrays wisdom's scorn as divine.

0:26.rendering: When terror comes like a storm, and your disaster arrives like a whirlwind, when distress and anguish overtake you.
0:26.note: The Hebrew word for "terror" here echoes the dread that falls on the wicked in the flood story, painting panic as an unstoppable force of nature.

0:27.rendering: Then they will call on me, but I will not answer. They will seek me eagerly, but they will not find me.
0:27.note: This verse echoes the urgent, repeated seeking language of Deuteronomy 4:29, flipping the promise of finding God into a haunting warning about ignored Wisdom.

0:28.rendering: Because they hated knowledge and did not choose the fear of the Lord.
0:28.note: The Hebrew word for "choose" echoes the decisive forks in the road that run throughout Proverbs, showing wisdom as a deliberate path rather than an accident.

0:29.rendering: They would not accept my advice, and they despised all my correction.
0:29.note: The Hebrew pairs "advice" (from the root for counsel) with "correction" (from the root for discipline), echoing the book's opening call to heed wisdom's voice like a father training a son.

0:30.rendering: Therefore they will eat the fruit of their own way, and be filled with their own schemes.
0:30.note: The Hebrew word for "fruit" echoes the garden's forbidden produce, showing how we harvest the very choices we plant.

0:31.rendering: For the turning away of the naive will kill them, and the complacency of fools will destroy them.
0:31.note: The Hebrew word for "turning away" carries the idea of deliberate apostasy, a choice that quietly leads the naive to ruin.

0:32.rendering: But whoever listens to me will live in safety, and will be at ease, without fear of harm.
0:32.note: The Hebrew word for "safety" echoes the same term used for the secure refuge of the righteous in Psalm 16:9, inviting slow meditation on true rest amid ancient warnings of folly.

28:0.rendering: Whoever remains stiff-necked after many rebukes will suddenly be shattered beyond healing.
28:0.note: The Hebrew "stiff-necked" echoes the wilderness generation's stubborn rebellion in Exodus, showing how repeated warnings build to an irreversible breaking point.

28:1.rendering: When the righteous increase, the people rejoice, but when a wicked man rules, the people groan.
28:1.note: The Hebrew word for "rejoice" here echoes the same root used for Israel's shouts of joy at the exodus, contrasting true leadership with oppressive rule.

28:2.rendering: A man who loves wisdom makes his father glad, but a companion of prostitutes squanders his wealth.
28:2.note: The Hebrew word for "companion" here echoes the "strange woman" of earlier proverbs, contrasting two paths of inheritance: wisdom enriches the family line, while chasing easy pleasure scatters it.

28:3.rendering: A king brings stability to the land by justice, but a man who demands heavy taxes tears it down.
28:3.note: The Hebrew word for "tears it down" echoes the language of destroying strongholds, hinting that oppressive taxation doesn't just burden, it dismantles a society from within.

28:4.rendering: A man who flatters his neighbor spreads a net for his feet.
28:4.note: The Hebrew word for "flatters" (chalaq) means to make smooth, the same root used for slippery paths that cause falls, turning deceptive words into a hidden trap.

28:5.rendering: In the transgression of an evil man there is a snare, but the righteous one runs and is glad.
28:5.note: The Hebrew word for snare (mokesh) echoes the traps laid for animals in the wilderness, revealing how sin tangles the wicked while the righteous escape into joy.

28:6.rendering: The righteous one knows the legal claim of the poor, but the wicked one does not understand such knowledge.
28:6.note: The Hebrew word for "knows" here carries the weight of intimate, relational understanding, not just facts.

28:7.rendering: Scoffers set a city ablaze, but the wise turn away wrath.
28:7.note: The Hebrew word for "scoffers" (letzim) carries the idea of arrogant mockers who inflame conflict, contrasting sharply with the wise who calm it, an image that echoes through Proverbs like fire versus water.

28:8.rendering: If a wise person argues with a fool, the fool rages and mocks, and there is no rest.
28:8.note: The Hebrew word for "rages" echoes the storm-tossed sea in other proverbs, showing how folly turns every conversation into chaos.

28:9.rendering: Bloodthirsty men hate the blameless one, but the upright seek his life.
28:9.note: The Hebrew wordplay contrasts "men of blood" who despise the innocent with the upright who actively pursue his well-being, echoing the ancient idea that true justice protects the vulnerable.

28:10.rendering: A fool gives full vent to his spirit, but a wise person holds it back and calms it.
28:10.note: The Hebrew word for "spirit" here is ruach, the same term for wind or breath, so this verse pictures the fool as a storm that cannot be restrained while the wise know how to quiet the gale within.

28:11.rendering: If a ruler listens to lies, all his servants become wicked.
28:11.note: The Hebrew word for "listens" carries the sense of attentive hearing that shapes action, showing how a leader's ears can corrupt an entire community.

28:12.rendering: The poor and the oppressor meet together, the LORD gives light to the eyes of them both.
28:12.note: In Hebrew the word for "oppressor" carries the sense of "one who crushes," yet the verse insists Yahweh's light falls on both the crushed and the crusher.

28:13.rendering: A king who judges the poor with truth, his throne will be established forever.
28:13.note: The Hebrew word for "established" echoes God's promise to David in 2 Samuel 7, linking just rule over the vulnerable to an enduring dynasty.

28:14.rendering: The rod and rebuke give wisdom, but a child left to himself brings shame to his mother.
28:14.note: The Hebrew pairs "rod" with "rebuke" to show that discipline is both physical and verbal, forming character through loving correction rather than neglect.

28:15.rendering: When the wicked increase, rebellion multiplies, but the righteous will see their downfall.
28:15.note: The Hebrew word for "rebellion" (pesha) echoes the very first human revolt in Eden, showing how unchecked evil always breeds more of the same ancient defiance.

28:16.rendering: Correct your son, and he will give you rest. He will give delight to your soul.
28:16.note: The Hebrew word for "correct" carries the double sense of discipline and instruction, forming the child into one who brings shalom both to the parent and to the community.

28:17.rendering: Where there is no vision, the people cast off restraint, but the one who keeps the law, happy is he.
28:17.note: The Hebrew word for "vision" here means prophetic revelation, not personal goals, linking back to the need for God's word to hold a community together.

28:18.rendering: A servant will not be corrected by words alone, for though he understands, he will not respond.
28:18.note: The Hebrew word for "respond" echoes the same term for obedient answer in Proverbs 15:1 and 1 Samuel 3:10, showing correction requires more than talk.

28:19.rendering: Do you see someone hasty with their words? There is more hope for a fool than for them.
28:19.note: The Hebrew pairs this warning with the fool from verse 9, using the same root for "hasty" that elsewhere describes reckless speech that invites disaster.

28:20.rendering: Whoever pampers his servant from childhood will in the end find him a son.
28:20.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "pamper" (from a root meaning to indulge or bring up delicately) and the shocking outcome of the servant becoming like a "son" warns that unchecked leniency disrupts household order.

28:21.rendering: A man of hot anger stirs up conflict, and a man of wrath abounds in transgression.
28:21.note: The Hebrew word for "stir up" echoes the chaos of Genesis 1, showing how unchecked anger turns a life back toward disorder.

28:22.rendering: A person's pride will bring him low, but the humble in spirit will obtain honor.
28:22.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "pride" (gavah) with being "high" only to tumble low, echoing the upside-down pattern seen from Hannah's song to Mary's Magnificat.

28:23.rendering: The partner of a thief hates his own life. He hears the curse but says nothing.
28:23.note: The Hebrew word for "partner" here echoes the idea of someone who shares in the crime, yet the verse paints him as self-destructive for staying silent under the public curse.

28:24.rendering: The fear of man sets a snare, but the one who trusts in the LORD will be set on high.
28:24.note: The Hebrew word for "snare" echoes the traps laid for animals in the ancient world, showing how human approval can quietly capture us.

28:25.rendering: Many seek the ruler's face, but from the LORD a man gets justice.
28:25.note: The Hebrew word for "face" echoes the priestly blessing's longing for God's shining face, showing where true favor ultimately comes from.

28:26.rendering: An unjust person is an abomination to the righteous, and the one who walks uprightly is an abomination to the wicked.
28:26.note: This closing proverb flips the word "abomination" to show how the same quality that delights the righteous repels the wicked, and vice versa.

28:27.rendering: Where there is no vision the people cast off restraint, but the one who keeps the law is happy.
28:27.note: The Hebrew for "vision" here means prophetic revelation, echoing the crisis in 1 Samuel 3:1 when God's word was rare and chaos followed.

27:0.rendering: The wicked flee when no one pursues, but the righteous are bold as a lion.
27:0.note: This proverb uses a hunting image: the wicked act like skittish prey, while the righteous carry the fearless confidence of the top predator.

27:1.rendering: When a land rebels, it has many rulers, but a man of understanding and knowledge maintains order.
27:1.note: The Hebrew word for "rebels" echoes the same root used for Israel's wilderness rebellions, hinting that national chaos often begins with hearts that refuse to be governed.

27:2.rendering: A poor man who oppresses the weak is like a beating rain that leaves no food.
27:2.note: The Hebrew word for "oppresses" echoes the very violence a ruler should prevent, exposing how poverty can corrupt character just as easily as wealth.

27:3.rendering: Those who abandon the law praise the wicked, but those who keep the law contend with them.
27:3.note: The Hebrew word for "contend" echoes the courtroom imagery of earlier proverbs, portraying law-keepers as active defenders of justice rather than passive observers.

27:4.rendering: Evil people do not understand justice, but those who seek the Lord understand it fully.
27:4.note: The Hebrew pairs "do not understand" with its root in "discern," creating a wordplay that contrasts hardened rebels with seekers who gain moral clarity over time.

27:5.rendering: Better to be poor and walk in integrity than rich and crooked in two ways.
27:5.note: The Hebrew word for "crooked in two ways" literally means "twisted of two paths," painting the rich deceiver as someone trying to straddle incompatible roads at once.

27:6.rendering: Whoever keeps the law is a discerning son, but a companion of gluttons shames his father.
27:6.note: The Hebrew word for "discerning" (mevin) echoes the garden story's call to wisdom, contrasting a son shaped by torah with one ruined by reckless feasting.

27:7.rendering: Whoever multiplies his wealth by interest and profit gathers it for the one who is kind to the poor.
27:7.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "usury and increase" (neshekh v'tarbit) with the unexpected reversal: exploitative gain funnels straight into the hands of the generous.

27:8.rendering: If someone turns their ear from listening to the instruction, even their prayer is an abomination.
27:8.note: The word "abomination" echoes the strong language used for idolatry and ritual impurity in Leviticus, showing that refusing God's torah pollutes even the most sacred act of prayer.

27:9.rendering: Whoever leads the upright astray into an evil path will fall into his own pit, but the blameless will inherit what is good.
27:9.note: The Hebrew word for "pit" (shachat) echoes the trap imagery of Psalm 7 and 9, where the wicked dig a hole only to tumble into it themselves.

27:10.rendering: A rich man is wise in his own eyes, but a poor person with discernment sees through him.
27:10.note: The Hebrew word for "discernment" here echoes the very gift Solomon asked for in 1 Kings 3, quietly suggesting that true insight often travels with empty pockets rather than full ones.

27:11.rendering: When the righteous triumph, there is great glory, but when the wicked rise, people hide themselves.
27:11.note: The Hebrew word for "triumph" literally means to rejoice or exult; this proverb paints a picture of how a just society sparkles with open celebration while tyranny forces everyone into the shadows.

27:12.rendering: Whoever covers their transgressions will not prosper, but whoever confesses and forsakes them will find mercy.
27:12.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "cover" (kasa) echoes back to the mercy seat that literally "covered" Israel's sins in the tabernacle.

27:13.rendering: Blessed is the one who is always fearful, but the one who hardens his heart will fall into calamity.
27:13.note: The Hebrew word for "fearful" here echoes the fear of the Lord that begins wisdom, inviting a lifelong posture of humble alertness rather than self-assured rigidity.

27:14.rendering: A roaring lion or a charging bear, so is a wicked ruler over a poor people.
27:14.note: The Hebrew pairs the rare word for "charging" bear with the image of a ruler who "rushes" to devour the vulnerable, an ancient Near Eastern warning that predatory government turns the king into a beast.

27:15.rendering: A leader who lacks understanding is a great oppressor, but one who hates unjust gain will prolong his days.
27:15.note: The Hebrew word for "oppressor" echoes the term for one who crushes the poor, linking bad leadership directly to the exploitation warned against throughout Proverbs.

15:0.rendering: To humans belong the plans of the heart, but from Yahweh comes the answer of the tongue.
15:0.note: The Hebrew word for "answer" here echoes the tongue's response in Proverbs 15:23 and 18:23, showing how our words ultimately rest in God's hands.

15:1.rendering: All the ways of a person seem pure in their own eyes, but the LORD weighs the spirit.
15:1.note: The Hebrew word for "weigh" (tkn) echoes the scales of justice in ancient marketplaces, revealing a sharp contrast between self-deception and divine discernment.

15:2.rendering: Commit your works to the LORD, and your plans will be established.
15:2.note: The Hebrew word for "commit" literally means "roll" your deeds onto the Lord, echoing the image of stones rolled away in other biblical moments of trust.

15:3.rendering: Yahweh has made everything for its purpose, even the wicked for the day of trouble.
15:3.note: The Hebrew word for "purpose" also means "answer," hinting that every created thing, even the wicked, will ultimately respond to God's design on the day of reckoning.

15:4.rendering: Every arrogant heart is an abomination to the LORD, be assured it will not go unpunished.
15:4.note: The Hebrew pairs "high heart" with God's visceral disgust, linking pride to the very sins that defiled the temple in the ancient world.

15:5.rendering: By loyalty and faithfulness iniquity is atoned for, and by the fear of the Lord one turns away from evil.
15:5.note: Loyalty and faithfulness are the same Hebrew pair used to describe God's own character in Exodus 34:6, showing how humans reflect the divine pattern to repair what is broken.

15:6.rendering: When a person's ways please the LORD, he makes even their enemies to be at peace with them.
15:6.note: The Hebrew word for "peace" here echoes the shalom of covenant blessing, turning hostility into harmony through a life aligned with God's order.

15:7.rendering: Better a little with righteousness than great revenues with injustice.
15:7.note: The word for "better" here echoes the repeated "better...than" proverbs that train the heart to prize character over cash.

16:0.rendering: Better a dry morsel with quietness than a house full of feasting with strife.
16:0.note: The Hebrew word for "quietness" echoes the shalom of covenant peace, contrasting noisy abundance with simple harmony.

16:1.rendering: A wise servant will rule over a shameful son and will share the inheritance as one of the brothers.
16:1.note: The Hebrew word for "shameful" (mevish) plays on the idea of bringing public disgrace, turning family hierarchy upside down through character rather than birth order.

16:2.rendering: The crucible is for silver, and the furnace for gold, but the Lord tests hearts.
16:2.note: This proverb echoes the refining imagery of Zechariah 13:9 and Malachi 3:2-3, where God himself is the refiner who purifies his people like precious metal.

16:3.rendering: A wicked person listens eagerly to lips of harm, a liar gives ear to a tongue of destruction.
16:3.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "listens" and "gives ear" to the same root for "hearing," exposing how evil hearts tune themselves to destructive speech like radio dials.

16:4.rendering: Whoever mocks the poor insults his Maker, and whoever gloats over disaster will not go unpunished.
16:4.note: This proverb echoes the creation story, where every human bears God's image, making mockery of the vulnerable an attack on the Creator himself.

16:5.rendering: Children's children are the crown of the old, and the glory of children is their parents.
16:5.note: The Hebrew word for "crown" (atarah) evokes a royal wreath, portraying grandchildren as the visible splendor that honors the elderly in ancient family-centered culture.

16:6.rendering: Fine speech is not fitting for a fool, much less are lying lips for a ruler.
16:6.note: The Hebrew word for "fine speech" (yeter) also means "excess" or "leftovers," so this proverb cleverly says surplus eloquence doesn't suit a fool, and lies suit a leader even less.

16:7.rendering: A bribe seems like a magic stone to its giver; wherever he turns, he succeeds.
16:7.note: The Hebrew word for "bribe" here is also used for a precious gift or charm, creating a wry wordplay on how it magically opens doors in the ancient patronage system.

17:0.rendering: A man who isolates himself seeks his own desire, and quarrels against all sound wisdom.
17:0.note: The Hebrew word for "isolates" (yipared) carries the idea of breaking away or tearing oneself off, hinting that selfish separation is an act of violence against community and wisdom.

17:1.rendering: A fool takes no delight in understanding, but only in exposing his own opinion.
17:1.note: The Hebrew word for "understanding" (binah) here implies discernment that comes from listening, the very thing the fool refuses to do.

17:2.rendering: When wickedness comes, contempt comes also, and with dishonor comes disgrace.
17:2.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "wickedness" (rasha) with "contempt" (baz) and "dishonor" (qalon) with "disgrace," showing how one drags the other along like a shadow.

17:3.rendering: Deep waters are the words of a person's mouth, a bubbling brook, a fountain of wisdom.
17:3.note: The Hebrew word for "deep waters" echoes the primordial chaos of Genesis 1:2, turning human speech into a creative force that can either bring life-giving wisdom or murky confusion.

17:4.rendering: It is not good to show partiality to the guilty, or to push aside the innocent in a lawsuit.
17:4.note: This verse plays on the ancient legal imagery of "lifting the face" of the powerful while crushing the righteous, exposing how favoritism perverts the very scales of justice God demands in the Torah.

17:5.rendering: A fool's lips bring strife, and his mouth invites blows.
17:5.note: The Hebrew pairs "lips" with strife and "mouth" with actual beatings, showing how foolish words quickly escalate from conflict to violence.

17:6.rendering: A fool's mouth is his ruin, and his lips are a snare for his life.
17:6.note: The Hebrew word for snare (moqesh) echoes the traps laid for animals in the wilderness, turning the fool's own words into the hunter that catches him.

17:7.rendering: The words of a gossip are like choice morsels, they sink deep into the belly.
17:7.note: The Hebrew word for "morsels" is the same one used for rare delicacies in Proverbs 17:1, turning gossip into something we secretly crave like forbidden sweets.

15:8.rendering: A person's heart plans his way, but Yahweh directs his steps.
15:8.note: The Hebrew word for "directs" (kun) echoes God's sovereign ordering of creation in Genesis, showing even our best-laid plans rest in the hands of the same God who established the cosmos.

15:9.rendering: A divine decision is on the lips of a king, his mouth does not betray justice in judgment.
15:9.note: The Hebrew word for "decision" is literally "oracle," echoing how ancient kings were seen as mouthpieces of the gods, yet this wisdom book grounds true justice in Yahweh's character instead of royal divinity.

15:10.rendering: Honest scales and balances belong to the LORD, all the weights in the bag are his work.
15:10.note: The "bag" of weights echoes the merchants' tool kit in Leviticus 19:36 and Deuteronomy 25:13, grounding everyday marketplace honesty in God's own character as Creator.

15:11.rendering: Kings detest doing wrong, for a throne is established through righteousness.
15:11.note: The Hebrew word for "throne" (kisse) echoes the same term used for God's own seat of rule, hinting that human kingship is meant to reflect divine order.

15:12.rendering: Kings take pleasure in righteous lips, and they love the one who speaks what is right.
15:12.note: The Hebrew pairs "righteous lips" with "speaking upright things," echoing the king's ideal role as lover of justice from earlier proverbs like 16:12.

15:13.rendering: A king's rage is a messenger of death, but a wise person will appease it.
15:13.note: The Hebrew pairs "rage" (chemah) with "messenger of death" (mal'akhe-mavet), echoing the royal executioners who carried out ancient Near Eastern kings' wrath.

15:14.rendering: In the light of a king's face there is life, and his favor is like a cloud with spring rain.
15:14.note: The Hebrew pairs "light of face" with life-giving rain, echoing how ancient kings were seen as divine-like providers of fertility and order.

15:15.rendering: Getting wisdom is far better than gold. Getting understanding is to be chosen over silver.
15:15.note: The Hebrew pairs "get" with wisdom and understanding, echoing the urgent parental plea in Proverbs 4:5,7 to "get" insight at any cost.

27:16.rendering: A man burdened with the blood of a life will flee to the pit, let no one support him.
27:16.note: The Hebrew word for "pit" (bor) echoes the fate of the innocent blood of Abel in Genesis 4, where the ground itself cries out.

27:17.rendering: Whoever walks in integrity will be delivered, but whoever is crooked in his ways will fall all at once.
27:17.note: The Hebrew word for "crooked" (iqqesh) echoes the twisted paths of the fool in earlier proverbs, painting sudden collapse as the natural end of a life bent out of alignment.

27:18.rendering: Whoever works his land will have plenty of bread, but whoever chases empty dreams will have plenty of poverty.
27:18.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "plenty" (yisba) in both outcomes, showing how our pursuits shape our harvest in surprisingly parallel ways.

27:19.rendering: A faithful person will overflow with blessings, but one who hurries to get rich will not go unpunished.
27:19.note: The Hebrew word for "faithful" echoes the same term used for trustworthy stewards in Genesis 39 and Exodus 18, contrasting patient character with the anxious scramble for wealth.

27:20.rendering: To show partiality is not good, yet a man will transgress for a piece of bread.
27:20.note: The Hebrew word for "piece of bread" (pat-lechem) echoes the simple daily provision in Proverbs 22:9 and 31:27, exposing how even the smallest bribe can twist justice.

27:21.rendering: A stingy man hurries after wealth, and does not know that poverty will come upon him.
27:21.note: The Hebrew word for "stingy" literally means "evil of eye," an ancient image of someone with a greedy, envious gaze that blinds him to the coming crash.

27:22.rendering: Whoever corrects a person will later find favor, more than one who flatters with the tongue.
27:22.note: The Hebrew word for "corrects" carries the idea of sharp rebuke, showing that honest confrontation builds trust where smooth words erode it.

27:23.rendering: Whoever robs his father or his mother and says, "It is no transgression," is a companion to a man who destroys.
27:23.note: The Hebrew pairs this thief with a "man of destruction" (ish mashchit), the same phrase used for the destroying angel in Exodus 12:23.

16:8.rendering: Whoever covers over an offense promotes love, but whoever repeats a matter separates close friends.
16:8.note: The Hebrew word for "covers" echoes the priestly act of atonement, hinting that everyday forgiveness carries a sacred, reconciling power.

16:9.rendering: A rebuke sinks deeper into a person of understanding than a hundred lashes into a fool.
16:9.note: The Hebrew verb for "sinks deeper" is the same one used for an arrow striking its target, showing how wise words pierce the heart while physical blows bounce off the fool.

16:10.rendering: A rebel seeks only evil, so a cruel messenger will be sent against him.
16:10.note: The Hebrew word for "rebel" (meri) echoes the wilderness generation's stubborn defiance against God, hinting that unchecked rebellion invites harsh correction.

16:11.rendering: Better to meet a she-bear robbed of her cubs than a fool in his folly.
16:11.note: The Hebrew word for "folly" here (ewilut) carries the idea of moral madness, not mere stupidity, an image that echoes the dangerous unpredictability of a mother bear throughout the ancient Near East.

16:12.rendering: Whoever returns evil for good, evil will never depart from his house.
16:12.note: This proverb echoes the ancient Near Eastern ideal of reciprocity, warning that inverting covenant loyalty by repaying good with harm invites permanent disruption into one's household.

16:13.rendering: The beginning of strife is like releasing water: so stop the quarrel before it bursts out.
16:13.note: The Hebrew word for "beginning" carries the image of a tiny breach in a dam; one small release of water quickly becomes a flood, training us to spot and seal conflict early.

16:14.rendering: Acquitting the guilty and condemning the innocent, both are an abomination to the LORD.
16:14.note: The paired verbs here echo the exact language of Deuteronomy 25:1, where judges must do the opposite, revealing how this proverb indicts the perversion of justice itself.

16:15.rendering: Why does a fool have a price in his hand to buy wisdom, when he has no understanding?
16:15.note: The Hebrew word for "understanding" (lev, "heart") hints that true wisdom is not bought with cash but formed in the core of a person, something a fool lacks by definition.

17:8.rendering: One who is slack in his work is a brother to the one who destroys.
17:8.note: The Hebrew word for "slack" echoes the term for "one who ruins or destroys" in the very next line, revealing that half-hearted effort isn't neutral, it actively partners with chaos.

17:9.rendering: The name of the LORD is a strong tower, the righteous run into it and are safe.
17:9.note: The Hebrew word for "strong tower" (migdal oz) echoes the fortified cities of the ancient Near East, where people fled for refuge during raids.

17:10.rendering: The wealth of a rich person is his strong city, and like a high wall in his own imagination.
17:10.note: The Hebrew word for "imagination" (maskit) hints at a fantasy or mental image, exposing how riches create an illusory fortress rather than real security.

17:11.rendering: Before destruction a person's heart is haughty, but humility comes before honor.
17:11.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew root for "high" (gavah): pride lifts the heart up toward ruin, while humility lowers it toward true elevation.

17:12.rendering: To answer before listening, that is folly and shame to him.
17:12.note: The Hebrew word order puts "answer" first, then "before listening," spotlighting the rush to speak as the root folly.

17:13.rendering: A person's spirit will sustain him through sickness, but a crushed spirit, who can bear it?
17:13.note: The Hebrew word for "spirit" (ruach) echoes the breath of life in Genesis 2:7, showing how inner vitality either carries us or collapses under pressure.

17:14.rendering: The mind of the discerning acquires knowledge, and the ear of the wise seeks it out.
17:14.note: The Hebrew pairs "heart" (mind) that gets knowledge with an "ear" that actively hunts for it, a wisdom twist on how true understanding comes to those who listen intently.

17:15.rendering: A person's gift makes room for him, and brings him before the great.
17:15.note: In the ancient world of patronage and courts, this proverb observes how a well-timed present can open doors that status alone cannot, echoing the Hebrew wordplay on "gift" (mattan) clearing a path (yachah).

15:16.rendering: The highway of the upright turns aside from evil, the one who guards his way preserves his life.
15:16.note: This proverb pictures moral life as a literal road to avoid, using the same Hebrew verb for "turns aside" that describes how God or his people swerve away from danger in Exodus and Psalms.

15:17.rendering: Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.
15:17.note: The Hebrew wordplay pairs "pride" (ga'avah) with "high" (gavoah), literally picturing a tall spirit that topples over.

15:18.rendering: Better to be lowly of spirit with the poor than to divide plunder with the proud.
15:18.note: The Hebrew word for "lowly of spirit" echoes the same phrase used for the crushed and afflicted in Isaiah 57:15, where God himself dwells with them.

15:19.rendering: Whoever gives careful attention to a matter will find success, and whoever trusts in the LORD, how blessed is that one.
15:19.note: The Hebrew pairs "maskil" (insightful attention) with "dabar" (a matter or word), echoing how wisdom literature trains us to listen deeply before acting.

15:20.rendering: The wise of heart is called discerning, and sweetness of lips increases learning.
15:20.note: In Hebrew, "sweetness of lips" plays on the same root as "learning" (leqach), suggesting that gracious speech itself becomes a form of persuasive instruction.

15:21.rendering: Insight is a fountain of life to the one who has it, but the discipline of fools is folly.
15:21.note: The Hebrew pairs "fountain of life" (a phrase echoing Eden and the temple) with the surprising idea that a fool's very correction only multiplies his foolishness.

15:22.rendering: The heart of the wise makes his mouth intelligent, and on his lips he adds persuasive learning.
15:22.note: The Hebrew links "heart" and "mouth" in a single cause-effect flow: wise character doesn't just speak, it teaches the tongue how to instruct others with insight.

15:23.rendering: Pleasant words are a honeycomb, sweet to the taste and healing to the bones.
15:23.note: In the ancient world, honey was both daily food and medicine, a perfect image for words that nourish and mend from within.

27:24.rendering: A greedy person stirs up conflict, but one who trusts in the LORD will flourish.
27:24.note: The Hebrew pairs "wide of appetite" with strife, contrasting it with the one who trusts and "grows fat," an image of thriving abundance.

27:25.rendering: Whoever trusts in his own heart is a fool, but whoever walks in wisdom will be delivered.
27:25.note: The Hebrew word for "fool" here echoes the same term used for the naive youth in Proverbs 1 who ignores wisdom's call.

27:26.rendering: Whoever gives to the poor will lack nothing, but one who shuts his eyes to them will get many curses.
27:26.note: The Hebrew pairs "close his eyes" with receiving curses, contrasting the open-handed giver who finds no lack, an echo of the covenant blessings in Deuteronomy 15.

27:27.rendering: When the wicked rise, people hide themselves, but when they perish, the righteous increase.
27:27.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "hide" (yissateru) and "increase" (yirbu) mirrors the reversal of fortunes, echoing the rise-and-fall pattern seen in the stories of Joseph and Esther.

27:28.rendering: A man who hardens his neck after many rebukes will suddenly be shattered beyond healing.
27:28.note: The Hebrew word for "hardens his neck" echoes the stiff-necked Israel of Exodus, a posture of defiant resistance that invites irreversible ruin.

17:16.rendering: The first to state his case seems right, until his neighbor comes and cross-examines him.
17:16.note: In the ancient courtroom culture of Proverbs, this verse exposes how persuasive first impressions can be, training us in the wisdom of patient listening before judgment.

17:17.rendering: The lot puts an end to quarrels and decides between powerful contenders.
17:17.note: In the ancient world, casting lots was a trusted way to invite God's hidden hand into human disputes, echoing the high priest's Urim and Thummim.

17:18.rendering: A brother offended is harder to win back than a fortified city, and quarrels are like the bars of a citadel.
17:18.note: The Hebrew word for "offended" carries the sense of being broken or betrayed, turning sibling bonds into siege walls that demand more than mere words to breach.

17:19.rendering: From the fruit of a man's mouth his stomach is satisfied, he is filled with the yield of his lips.
17:19.note: The Hebrew pairs "mouth" and "lips" in a tight echo of how words produce tangible results, like a tree yielding harvest.

17:20.rendering: Death and life are in the power of the tongue, and those who love it will eat its fruit.
17:20.note: The Hebrew word for "power" here is yad, literally "hand," evoking how speech grips and shapes reality like a tool.

17:21.rendering: Whoever finds a wife finds a good thing, and obtains favor from the LORD.
17:21.note: The Hebrew verb "finds" echoes the man "finding" a helper in Genesis 2:20, portraying marriage as a joyful discovery laced with divine kindness.

17:22.rendering: The poor speaks with pleading words, but the rich answers with harshness.
17:22.note: In ancient wisdom, this proverb exposes how power shapes speech: the vulnerable must beg while the secure can afford to be rude.

17:23.rendering: A man of many companions may come to ruin, but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother.
17:23.note: The Hebrew word for "companion" here echoes the earlier warnings about bad company in Proverbs, while the rare phrase "sticks closer" pictures glue-like loyalty that outdoes even family bonds.

16:16.rendering: A friend loves at all times, and a sibling is born for times of trouble.
16:16.note: The Hebrew word for "friend" here echoes the covenant language of loyal love used for God himself in the Torah.

16:17.rendering: A person lacking sense gives a pledge, and becomes security for his neighbor's debt.
16:17.note: The Hebrew word for "lacking sense" is the same one used for the naive youth in Proverbs 1 who needs wisdom's urgent call.

16:18.rendering: Whoever loves rebellion loves a fight, and whoever builds his doorway high invites a collapse.
16:18.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "loves transgression" with "loves strife," then pictures a haughty gate that literally invites its own breaking down.

16:19.rendering: A crooked heart will not find good, and a twisted tongue will fall into disaster.
16:19.note: The Hebrew pairs "crooked heart" with "twisted tongue," showing how inner distortion inevitably leaks out in speech and leads to ruin.

16:20.rendering: A fool gives birth to grief for himself, and the father of a scoundrel has no joy.
16:20.note: The Hebrew wordplay links the "fool" (kesil) directly to the grief (ka'as) he produces, like a twisted family resemblance.

16:21.rendering: A joyful heart is good medicine, but a crushed spirit dries up the bones.
16:21.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "joyful heart" (lev sameach) to the root for "healing," while a "crushed spirit" literally "dries the bones" like a desert wind.

16:22.rendering: A wicked person accepts a bribe from the bosom, to pervert the paths of justice.
16:22.note: The Hebrew says the bribe comes "from the bosom," the fold in a garment where one hid secret payments, an image of sly, intimate corruption.

16:23.rendering: Wisdom is right in front of the discerning person's face, but the eyes of a fool roam to the ends of the earth.
16:23.note: The Hebrew word for "face" (panim) stands in witty contrast to the fool's eyes that wander to the "end of the earth," showing how wisdom stays near while folly chases distant distractions.

15:24.rendering: There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way to death.
15:24.note: The Hebrew word for "way" (derek) echoes the two paths of wisdom and folly that run from Proverbs 1 all the way through chapter 9.

15:25.rendering: A worker's appetite works for him; his mouth urges him on.
15:25.note: The Hebrew plays on *nephesh* (appetite/throat/soul) driving a person like an inner taskmaster, an ancient nod to how hunger itself becomes the boss that gets work done.

15:26.rendering: A worthless person digs up evil, and on his lips is a scorching fire.
15:26.note: The Hebrew paints the scoundrel as one who "digs up" trouble like buried treasure, then sets it ablaze with his words.

15:27.rendering: A crooked person stirs up conflict, and a gossip separates close friends.
15:27.note: The Hebrew word for "gossip" here (nirgan) literally means "whisperer," evoking the ancient image of someone whose quiet words fracture even the strongest alliances.

15:28.rendering: A violent person lures his neighbor, and leads him down a path that is not good.
15:28.note: The Hebrew word for "lures" is the same one used for a prostitute seducing a young man in Proverbs 7, exposing how violence and greed share the same deceptive playbook.

15:29.rendering: Whoever winks with their eyes plots harm, and whoever purses their lips brings evil to pass.
15:29.note: The Hebrew verbs here carry a sense of completion, showing how a mere gesture, like a wink or lip-purse, can hatch and finish evil.

15:30.rendering: Gray hair is a crown of glory, it is found in the way of righteousness.
15:30.note: In the ancient world, gray hair signaled wisdom earned through long experience, but this proverb ties that honor directly to a life of righteousness.

15:31.rendering: Better a patient person than a warrior, and one who rules their own spirit than one who captures a city.
15:31.note: The Hebrew word for "rule" echoes the dominion language of Genesis 1, redirecting conquest from outward battles to the inner battlefield of the self.

15:32.rendering: The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the Lord.
15:32.note: In the ancient world, casting lots was the standard way to seek divine will, yet this proverb boldly claims God sovereignly directs even that random act.

16:24.rendering: A foolish son brings grief to his father, and bitterness to the one who bore him.
16:24.note: The Hebrew pairs "grief" (ka'as) with the mother who "bore" him, echoing the pain of childbirth itself turned backward.

16:25.rendering: To punish the innocent is not good, nor to strike the noble for his uprightness.
16:25.note: The Hebrew word for "strike" (nakah) echoes the language of judicial beating in Deuteronomy 25, exposing how perverting justice against the righteous twists the very law meant to protect them.

16:26.rendering: Whoever restrains his words has knowledge, and a cool spirit is a person of understanding.
16:26.note: The Hebrew pairs "restrains his words" with "cool of spirit," using an idiom for calm self-control that echoes the hot-tempered fool in Proverbs 14:17.

16:27.rendering: Even a fool who keeps silent is considered wise, and the one who shuts his lips is esteemed as having discernment.

26:0.rendering: Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day will bring forth.
26:0.note: The Hebrew word for "boast" (hithallel) carries the idea of praising oneself, a subtle warning against assuming control over a future that belongs to God alone.

26:1.rendering: Let a stranger praise you, and not your own mouth, a foreigner, and not your own lips.
26:1.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "mouth" and "lips" echoes the earlier warnings against self-flattery in Proverbs, training us to crave honest outside eyes over self-promotion.

26:2.rendering: A stone is heavy and sand is weighty, but a fool's provocation is heavier than both of them.
26:2.note: The Hebrew word for "provocation" (kasaf) plays on the idea of anger as a crushing burden, turning the fool's rage into something that literally weighs down everyone around him.

26:3.rendering: Cruelty is fierce and anger is a flood, but who can stand before jealousy?
26:3.note: The Hebrew word for jealousy here carries a scorching heat, echoing the same fire that consumed Joseph's brothers in Genesis 37 and warns how envy outlasts even rage in its power to destroy relationships.

26:4.rendering: Better is open rebuke than hidden love.
26:4.note: The Hebrew wordplay pits the visible wound of rebuke against love that stays concealed, revealing how true friendship risks discomfort for the other's good.

26:5.rendering: Faithful are the wounds of a friend, but the kisses of an enemy are excessive.
26:5.note: The Hebrew word for "excessive" (literally "profuse" or "abundant") ironically twists the enemy's kisses into something overdone and false, contrasting the healing pain of true friendship.

26:6.rendering: A satisfied appetite loathes honey from the comb, but to a hungry stomach every bitter thing is sweet.
26:6.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "sweet" (matok) and "bitter" (mar) highlights how desperate hunger transforms even the harshest realities into welcome relief, an ancient observation on perspective shaped by need.

26:7.rendering: Like a bird that strays from its nest is a person who strays from his home.
26:7.note: The Hebrew word for "strays" repeats to echo the bird's restless wandering, painting exile from home as a kind of self-imposed homelessness.

25:0.rendering: Like snow in summer or rain in harvest, so honor is not fitting for a fool.
25:0.note: The Hebrew pairs two impossibilities in the agricultural calendar, snow ruining summer crops and rain destroying the harvest, to expose how out of place public honor is for a fool.

25:1.rendering: Like a fluttering sparrow or a darting swallow, an undeserved curse does not alight.
25:1.note: The Hebrew word for "alight" is the same one used for a bird landing on its nest, painting the curse as something restless that cannot settle where it doesn't belong.

25:2.rendering: A whip for the horse, a bridle for the donkey, and a rod for the back of fools.
25:2.note: The Hebrew word for "rod" echoes the same term used for disciplining a son in Proverbs 13:24 and 22:15, linking animal training to the formation of human character.

25:3.rendering: Do not answer a fool according to his folly, or you too will be like him.
25:3.note: This proverb's punch is in the wordplay on "answer" and "folly" (both from the root for speaking and stupidity), warning that stooping to a fool's level drags you into the same mud.

25:4.rendering: Answer a fool according to his folly, or he will be wise in his own eyes.
25:4.note: This pairs with the previous verse's warning not to answer a fool, creating a deliberate paradox that trains the reader in discerning wisdom on the spot.

25:5.rendering: Sending a message by the hand of a fool is like cutting off one's own feet and drinking down violence.
25:5.note: The Hebrew word for "violence" (hamas) echoes the chaotic bloodshed of Genesis 6:11, turning a bad decision into self-inflicted catastrophe.

25:6.rendering: Like the legs of a lame person that hang limp, so is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
25:6.note: The Hebrew word for "legs" also means "proverbs," creating a pun that makes the fool's wise words as useless as dangling, non-functional limbs.

25:7.rendering: Like tying a stone into a sling, so is giving honor to a fool.
25:7.note: The Hebrew paints a picture of a fool's

24:0.rendering: These also are proverbs of Solomon, which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied out.
24:0.note: Hezekiah's scribes, 250 years after Solomon, curated and preserved these sayings, showing how wisdom literature grew as a living tradition in Judah's royal court.

24:1.rendering: It is the glory of God to conceal a matter, but the glory of kings is to search out a matter.
24:1.note: This proverb delights in the tension between divine mystery and human investigation, inviting wisdom as a royal pursuit of hidden truth.

24:2.rendering: The heavens for height, and the earth for depth, and the heart of kings is unsearchable.
24:2.note: This proverb echoes the creation imagery of Genesis 1, linking cosmic vastness to the mysterious, layered mind of a ruler.

24:3.rendering: Remove the dross from the silver, and the smith has material for a vessel.
24:3.note: This ancient metallurgical image plays on the Hebrew word for "dross" (sigim) that echoes the refining fire of earlier wisdom texts like Proverbs 17:3.

24:4.rendering: Remove the wicked from the king's presence, and his throne will be established in righteousness.
24:4.note: The Hebrew word for "remove" echoes the language of purging dross from silver in the previous verse, picturing the king as a refiner who must clear evil influencers to establish a just reign.

24:5.rendering: Do not exalt yourself in the king's presence, and do not stand in the place of the great.
24:5.note: This proverb echoes the ancient court etiquette of the Near East, where overstepping social rank could bring public humiliation.

24:6.rendering: It is better for him to say to you, "Come up here," than for him to humble you before a noble, what your eyes have seen.
24:6.note: This proverb flips the ancient honor-shame game on its head, echoing Jesus' later table teaching in Luke 14:7-11 about taking the lowest seat.

24:7.rendering: Do not hastily bring a lawsuit. What will you do in the end when your neighbor puts you to shame?
24:7.note: The Hebrew verb for "bring a lawsuit" echoes the same root used for "quarrel" in Proverbs 17:14, linking personal disputes to the danger of an uncontainable flood.

26:8.rendering: Oil and incense bring joy to the heart, and the sweetness of a friend comes from his heartfelt counsel.
26:8.note: The Hebrew pairs "oil and incense" with a friend's counsel, evoking the temple's sacred anointing and fragrant offerings as an image of intimate, life-giving relationship.

26:9.rendering: Do not abandon your own friend or your father's friend, and do not go to your brother's house in the day of your calamity. Better is a neighbor nearby than a brother far away.
26:9.note: The proverb plays on the Hebrew word for “near” (qarob), contrasting physical distance with relational closeness in a crisis.

26:10.rendering: Be wise, my son, and make my heart glad, so I can answer the one who mocks me.
26:10.note: The Hebrew word for "wise" echoes the book's opening call in Proverbs 1:2, turning personal growth into a public defense against scorn.

26:11.rendering: A shrewd person sees danger coming and hides, but the naive keep going and pay the price.
26:11.note: The Hebrew word for "shrewd" (arum) echoes the serpent in Eden, turning cunning into a tool for wise self-preservation rather than rebellion.

26:12.rendering: Take his garment, for he has given security for a stranger, and hold it in pledge for a foreign woman.
26:12.note: This proverb cleverly echoes the ancient practice of seizing collateral from a reckless guarantor, using the same Hebrew root for "pledge" found in Exodus 22 to warn against foolish financial entanglements.

26:13.rendering: Whoever blesses his neighbor with a loud voice early in the morning, it will be counted as a curse to him.
26:13.note: The Hebrew word for "blessing" here carries ironic bite: what looks like pious goodwill becomes social sabotage when it shatters sleep.

26:14.rendering: A constant dripping on a rainy day and a quarrelsome wife are alike.
26:14.note: The Hebrew word for “quarrelsome” (midyanim) sounds like the word for “constant dripping” (daled-nun), turning the proverb into a playful, onomatopoeic pun that makes you hear the annoyance.

26:15.rendering: Restraining the wind and grasping oil with the right hand, so is one who restrains a quarrelsome wife.
26:15.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "wind" (ruach) and "oil" (shemen) to the slippery, uncontainable nature of a contentious spouse, an ancient image of futility.

23:0.rendering: Do not be envious of evil men, and do not desire to be with them.
23:0.note: The Hebrew word for "envy" here echoes its use in the tenth commandment, warning that craving the violent shortcuts of the wicked will pull you into their company and their crash.

23:1.rendering: For their hearts plot violence, and their lips talk about trouble.
23:1.note: The Hebrew pairs "heart" (lev) with plotting ruin and "lips" (sefatayim) with murmuring trouble, exposing how inner scheming leaks out in speech.

23:2.rendering: A house is built by wisdom, and it is established by understanding.
23:2.note: The Hebrew word for "house" echoes the temple and royal palace, hinting that true stability, whether for a family or a kingdom, comes from skillful wisdom rather than brute strength.

23:3.rendering: and by knowledge the rooms are filled with all precious and pleasant riches.
23:3.note: The Hebrew word for "knowledge" here echoes its use in Proverbs 1:7, the book's foundational motto, showing that a stable house begins with the fear of the Lord.

23:4.rendering: A wise person is strong, and a person of knowledge increases his power.
23:4.note: The Hebrew pairs "wise" with "strength" and "knowledge" with "might," subverting the ancient warrior ideal by claiming brains outlast brawn.

23:5.rendering: For by wise guidance you will wage your war, and victory comes with an abundance of counselors.
23:5.note: The Hebrew word for "war" here echoes the same term used for the battles Joshua and David fought, turning everyday decisions into strategic campaigns best won with many advisors.

23:6.rendering: Wisdom is too high for a fool, in the gate he does not open his mouth.
