23:6.note: In the city gate where elders debated justice and business, the fool stays silent, revealing how character, not just knowledge, determines who belongs in the public square.

23:7.rendering: Whoever plots to do evil will be called a master schemer.
23:7.note: The Hebrew word for "plots" is the same root used for the craftsman's skillful planning in Exodus, turning a positive skill into a warning about twisted wisdom.

24:8.rendering: When you argue your case with your neighbor, do not reveal another's secret.
24:8.note: The Hebrew word for "argue your case" (rib) is the same legal term used for covenant lawsuits between God and Israel, turning everyday neighbor disputes into echoes of divine justice.

24:9.rendering: lest one who hears it shame you, and your ill repute never depart.
24:9.note: The verse warns that a private dispute dragged into public court risks permanent loss of face, an ancient Near Eastern horror where reputation was life itself.

24:10.rendering: A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in settings of silver.
24:10.note: The Hebrew for "fitly" plays on "wheel" or "turning," evoking a word that arrives at exactly the right moment, like a gleaming ornament catching the eye.

24:11.rendering: A gold ring or an ornament of fine gold, so is a wise rebuker to a listening ear.
24:11.note: The Hebrew pairs the rare word for "wise rebuker" with imagery of exquisite jewelry, turning correction into something beautiful and valuable when received.

24:12.rendering: Like the cold of snow in the heat of harvest, so is a trustworthy messenger to those who sent him: he revives his masters' spirit.
24:12.note: The Hebrew word for "revives" echoes the same root used for the breath of life in Genesis 2, portraying a faithful messenger as one who brings refreshing life to his senders.

24:13.rendering: Like clouds and wind without rain is a man who boasts of a gift he never gives.
24:13.note: The Hebrew word for "boast" here also means to praise or lift up, creating a sharp irony: empty praise that promises blessing but delivers drought.

24:14.rendering: Through patient persistence a ruler is persuaded, and a soft tongue can break a bone.
24:14.note: The Hebrew pairs "long nose" (patient anger) with a "soft tongue" to shatter bone, a striking image of gentle speech wielding greater power than force.

24:15.rendering: If you find honey, eat only what you need, or you will get sick of it and vomit it up.
24:15.note: This proverb uses the concrete image of overeating honey to train the virtue of moderation in all good things.

25:8.rendering: Like a thornbush that a drunkard waves around, so is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
25:8.note: The Hebrew word for “thorn” (ḥo’aḥ) also appears in Judges 9’s bramble fable, linking this image of useless, painful chaos to a story about unworthy leadership.

25:9.rendering: Like an archer who wounds everyone, so is one who hires a fool or hires those who pass by.
25:9.note: The Hebrew wordplay links the "wounding" archer with the employer who hires the unqualified or transient, both scattering harm without aim.

25:10.rendering: Like a dog that returns to its vomit, a fool repeats his folly.
25:10.note: This vivid image, echoed in 2 Peter 2:22, paints the fool's stubborn return to self-destructive patterns as something even animals find repulsive.

25:11.rendering: Do you see a man wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
25:11.note: The Hebrew word for "fool" here is the same one used for the sluggard in verse 15, linking self-deceived pride with laziness of mind.

25:12.rendering: The lazy one says, "There's a lion in the street! A lion right in the middle of the roads!"
25:12.note: This proverb mocks the absurd excuses of the sluggard, painting him as so inventive in his fear that he imagines lions prowling the city squares.

25:13.rendering: As a door turns on its hinges, so a sluggard turns on his bed.
25:13.note: The Hebrew word for “turns” repeats, painting the lazy person as a door that never goes anywhere, just pivots in place.

25:14.rendering: The lazy one buries his hand in the dish, too weary even to lift it back to his mouth.
25:14.note: This vivid image of a sluggard too exhausted to eat plays on the same Hebrew root for "bury" and "weary" found in verse 14's door that turns on its hinges, linking physical laziness to total inaction.

25:15.rendering: The sluggard is wiser in his own eyes than seven people who can answer with good sense.
25:15.note: Ancient wisdom often used the number seven for complete perfection, so the lazy person imagines himself smarter than the totality of sound judgment.

26:16.rendering: Iron sharpens iron, and one person sharpens the face of another.
26:16.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "iron" (barzel) with the verb "to sharpen" (yachad), evoking the ancient blacksmith's fire and hammer that transform raw metal.

26:17.rendering: Whoever tends a fig tree will eat its fruit, and whoever guards his master will be honored.
26:17.note: The Hebrew word for "guards" echoes the verb used for Adam "keeping" the garden in Genesis 2, linking faithful service to the ancient ideal of fruitful stewardship.

26:18.rendering: As water reflects the face, so the heart of a person reflects the person.
26:18.note: The Hebrew word for "face" (panim) echoes in "heart reflects the man," a wordplay that suggests your inner self stares back at you like a still pool.

26:19.rendering: Sheol and Destruction are never satisfied, and a human's eyes are never satisfied either.
26:19.note: The Hebrew pairs Sheol and Abaddon as twin insatiable forces, then shocks us by equating them with the human gaze, an ancient warning about endless craving.

26:20.rendering: The crucible is for silver, and the furnace for gold, but a man is tested by his praise.
26:20.note: The Hebrew word for "praise" (maḥălāl) plays on the same root as "crucible" (maṣrēp), revealing that public acclaim is the ultimate refining fire for character.

26:21.rendering: Though you pound a fool in a mortar with a pestle along with crushed grain, his folly will not depart from him.
26:21.note: The mortar and pestle echo the daily grinding of manna in the wilderness, showing that no amount of external pressure can remove deep-seated folly.

26:22.rendering: Know well the face of your flock, give careful attention to your herds.
26:22.note: This proverb echoes the ancient Near Eastern ideal of the shepherd-king who inspects his animals personally, a concrete image of attentive leadership over any entrusted responsibility.

26:23.rendering: For riches do not last forever, nor does a crown endure from generation to generation.
26:23.note: The Hebrew pairs "treasure" (otsar) with "crown" (nezer), hinting that even royal wealth and status are as fleeting as grass.

23:8.rendering: The scheming of a fool is sin, and a scoffer is an abomination to humanity.
23:8.note: The Hebrew pairs the fool's "plan" (zimmah, often a dark scheme) with the scoffer as something that stinks to the core of human community.

23:9.rendering: If you slack off in the day of trouble, your strength is small indeed.
23:9.note: The Hebrew word for "trouble" (tsarah) echoes the same term used for the pressure that reveals true character, like a refiner's fire.

23:10.rendering: Rescue those who are being dragged off to death, and hold back those who are stumbling toward the slaughter.
23:10.note: The Hebrew verbs here paint a picture of urgent physical intervention, like grabbing someone by the arm right before they topple over a cliff.

23:11.rendering: If you say, "Look, we did not know," does not the one who weighs hearts perceive it? And the one who watches over your soul, will he not know? And will he not repay each person according to what he has done?
23:11.note: The phrase "weighs hearts" echoes the Egyptian idea of divine judgment but here points to Yahweh as the true heart-weigher who sees through every excuse.

23:12.rendering: My son, eat honey, for it is good, and honeycomb drips sweet to your palate.
23:12.note: In the ancient world, wild honey represented rare, God-given delight, training the young in wise discernment between true sweetness and deceptive pleasure.

23:13.rendering: Know that wisdom is like this for your soul: if you find it, there will be a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
23:13.note: The Hebrew links wisdom directly to the soul's future, echoing the honey metaphor in verse 13 as something both sweet and sustaining.

23:14.rendering: Do not lie in wait like a bandit against the home of the righteous, do not assault his resting place.
23:14.note: The Hebrew verb for "lie in wait" echoes the same word used for the adulteress stalking her prey in Proverbs 7, turning the image of predatory ambush into a warning against envy-driven violence.

23:15.rendering: For a righteous person falls seven times and rises again, but the wicked are brought down by calamity.
23:15.note: The Hebrew word for "falls" echoes the same root used when the wicked stumble in verse 17, linking the two ideas through wordplay.

24:16.rendering: Let your foot be seldom in your neighbor's house, lest he become weary of you and hate you.
24:16.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "hate" (sane), flipping the expected hospitality code of ancient Near Eastern villages into a witty warning against overstaying your welcome.

24:17.rendering: Like a club, a sword, and a sharpened arrow is a man who testifies falsely against his neighbor.
24:17.note: The Hebrew piles up three lethal weapons in rapid succession, turning the courtroom liar into an armed assailant.

24:18.rendering: A broken tooth or a slipping foot: so is confidence in a traitor on a day of trouble.
24:18.note: The Hebrew word for "confidence" here echoes the root for "to trust," turning betrayal into a proverb about unreliable support that literally "gives way" like a bad tooth or foot.

24:19.rendering: Like taking off a coat on a cold day, or like pouring vinegar on a wound, is singing songs to a troubled heart.
24:19.note: The Hebrew word for "troubled" (ra') can also mean "evil" or "bitter," creating a wordplay: singing cheerful songs over a bitter soul is as jarring and painful as vinegar on an open wound.

24:20.rendering: If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat, and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink.
24:20.note: This proverb echoes the ancient Near Eastern wisdom of returning good for hostility, an idea Jesus later echoes in the Sermon on the Mount.

24:21.rendering: For you will heap burning coals on his head, and the Lord will reward you.
24:21.note: The "burning coals" image echoes a striking ancient Near Eastern gesture of carrying a brazier of live coals on the head as a sign of remorse and repentance.

24:22.rendering: The north wind brings forth rain, and a sly tongue brings an angry face.
24:22.note: This proverb uses the Hebrew word for "brings forth" (chul) in both halves, linking weather patterns to the predictable effect of gossip on relationships.

24:23.rendering: Better to live in a corner of the housetop than in a house shared with a quarrelsome wife.
24:23.note: The Hebrew word for "quarrelsome" echoes the dripping rain in verse 15, painting a picture of constant, wearing irritation.

26:24.rendering: When the hay is taken and the new growth appears, and the grass from the hills is gathered in,
26:24.note: This verse paints a farmer's rhythm of harvest and regrowth, echoing how wisdom gathers provision in its proper season.

24:24.rendering: Like cold water to a weary throat is good news from a distant land.
24:24.note: The Hebrew word for "good news" (besorah) is the same root the New Testament will later use for gospel, turning this proverb into a meditation on how distant hope revives the soul.

23:16.rendering: Do not rejoice when your enemy falls, and do not let your heart be glad when he stumbles,
23:16.note: This proverb echoes the ancient Near Eastern wisdom against gloating, training the heart in empathy even toward adversaries.

23:17.rendering: lest the LORD see it and be displeased, and turn away his anger from him.
23:17.note: This verse warns against gloating over an enemy's fall, because the Lord, who sees the heart's delight in another's pain, may redirect his discipline onto the gloating one instead.

23:18.rendering: Do not get worked up about evildoers, do not be jealous of the wicked,
23:18.note: The Hebrew verb here for "get worked up" is the same one used in Psalm 37:1, creating an intertextual echo that links wisdom's calm to the life of trusting Yahweh amid injustice.

23:19.rendering: For the evil have no future, the lamp of the wicked will be snuffed out.
23:19.note: The Hebrew word for "future" (acharit) echoes the end of the wicked in Proverbs 23:18, painting their snuffed-out lamp as a picture of final, total darkness.

23:20.rendering: My son, fear the LORD and the king, and do not associate with those who are given to change.
23:20.note: The Hebrew pairs "Yahweh and the king" as twin authorities, an ancient Near Eastern idea that loyalty to both guards against rebels who shift allegiances like changing clothes.

23:21.rendering: For their disaster will arise suddenly, and who knows the ruin that will come from them both?
23:21.note: The Hebrew pairs "their disaster" with a sudden fall, echoing the paired warnings against the king and God in verse 21, like twin dangers that can topple the rebellious in an instant.

25:16.rendering: Whoever grabs a dog by the ears is like the one who meddles in a quarrel that is not his own.
25:16.note: The Hebrew word for “meddles” literally means “to be aroused or excited over” a fight that doesn’t belong to you, mirroring the reckless danger of seizing a street dog’s ears.

25:17.rendering: Like a madman who hurls flaming arrows, arrows of death, so is the person who deceives his neighbor and then says, "I was only joking."
25:17.note: The Hebrew word for "madman" here is the same one used for a raving prophet, hinting that reckless words can mimic dangerous inspiration.

25:18.rendering: so is a person who deceives his neighbor and then says, "I was only joking."
25:18.note: The Hebrew verb for "deceive" (r-m-h) is the same one used when Jacob tricks Isaac, showing how "just kidding" often masks real harm.

25:19.rendering: Without wood a fire goes out, and without a whisperer conflict dies down.
25:19.note: The Hebrew pairs "wood" and "whisperer" with the same root for kindling, showing how gossip literally feeds strife like fuel on embers.

25:20.rendering: Just as charcoal for embers and wood for fire, so a quarrelsome person kindles strife.
25:20.note: The Hebrew word for "quarrelsome" (madon) echoes the name of the place where Israel first quarreled with God in the wilderness, turning small sparks into national disaster.

25:21.rendering: The words of a gossip are like choice morsels, they go down into the innermost parts of the belly.
25:21.note: In Hebrew the phrase "go down to the chambers of the belly" echoes Proverbs 18:8 and 20:30, picturing how slander lodges deep and shapes us from the inside.

25:22.rendering: Fervent lips and a wicked heart are like a silver glaze overlaid on a clay pot.
25:22.note: The Hebrew wordplay here links "burning lips" with "burning silver dross," exposing how smooth talk can thinly coat a corrupt heart like cheap pottery pretending to be fine silver.

25:23.rendering: With his lips a hater disguises himself, but inside he sets deceit.
25:23.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "lips" (outer speech) and "belly" (inner self) exposes how hatred hides in plain sight.

25:24.rendering: When he speaks graciously, do not believe him, for there are seven abominations in his heart.
25:24.note: The "seven abominations" echoes the list in Proverbs 6:16-19, painting the deceiver as a walking catalog of everything God hates.

25:25.rendering: Though hatred covers itself with deception, wickedness will be exposed in the assembly.
25:25.note: The Hebrew word for "covers" (k-s-h) plays against the idea of public exposure in the congregation, echoing how wisdom literature loves to watch evil's clever disguises unravel in community.

25:26.rendering: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it, and whoever rolls a stone will have it roll back on them.
25:26.note: This proverb plays on the image of ancient stone-rolling traps and pits for animals or enemies, showing how schemes boomerang in the wisdom order of creation.

25:27.rendering: A lying tongue hates those it crushes, and a flattering mouth works ruin.
25:27.note: The Hebrew verb for "crushes" echoes the word for "shatter" in Psalm 2, where the Messiah breaks the nations with a rod of iron.

18:0.rendering: Better a poor person who walks in integrity than a fool whose lips are twisted with lies.
18:0.note: The Hebrew word for "integrity" (tom) echoes the blameless walk of Genesis 6:9 and 17:1, contrasting the fool's crooked speech with the wise path that leads to life.

18:1.rendering: Desire without knowledge is not good, and whoever hurries his feet misses the way.
18:1.note: The Hebrew pairs "soul without knowledge" with hasty feet, exposing how unchecked passion often outruns wisdom and lands us off the path.

18:2.rendering: A person's own foolishness twists his way, yet his heart rages against the LORD.
18:2.note: The Hebrew word for "twists" echoes the same root used for a perverted or crooked path elsewhere in Proverbs, showing how self-inflicted chaos leads to misplaced blame.

18:3.rendering: Wealth adds many friends, but the poor is separated even from his close neighbor.
18:3.note: The Hebrew word for "adds" echoes the verb used when God added to creation, hinting that money artificially multiplies relationships while poverty strips them away.

18:4.rendering: A false witness will not go unpunished, and one who breathes out lies will not escape.
18:4.note: The phrase "breathes out lies" echoes the vivid Hebrew image of a witness who exhales deceit like poison, linking back to the Ten Commandments' warning against false testimony.

18:5.rendering: Many seek the favor of a noble, and everyone is a friend to a giver of gifts.
18:5.note: The Hebrew word for "favor" here is the same one used for the face of God in priestly blessing: many chase a powerful person's shining face, but only the generous truly wins loyal friends.

18:6.rendering: All the relatives of a poor man hate him, how much more do his friends go far from him. He pursues them with words, but they are not there.
18:6.note: The Hebrew plays on the word for "pursue" (radaf), echoing how the poor man chases friends the way hunters chase prey, yet they vanish.

18:7.rendering: Whoever gets wisdom loves his own life, and whoever guards understanding will find good.
18:7.note: The Hebrew pairs "heart" (leb) with wisdom here, showing that true insight isn't just head knowledge but a heart-level affection that protects your very self.

18:8.rendering: A false witness will not go unpunished, and one who breathes out lies will perish.
18:8.note: The Hebrew pairs "false witness" with "breathes out lies," echoing the vivid imagery of Genesis 2:7 where God breathes life into humanity, now twisted into deadly deceit.

18:9.rendering: Luxury is not fitting for a fool, much less for a slave to rule over princes.
18:9.note: The Hebrew pairs two upside-down social orders with the same word for "fitting" or "proper," exposing how wisdom sees both as equally grotesque violations of created order.

18:10.rendering: Good sense makes a person slow to anger, and it is his glory to overlook an offense.
18:10.note: The word for "glory" (tiph'eret) is the same one used for the splendor of a crown or the beauty of the temple, turning restraint into a visible crown of wisdom.

18:11.rendering: A king's rage is like a lion's roar, but his favor is like dew on the grass.
18:11.note: The Hebrew wordplay links the king's terrifying growl to a lion's roar, yet his pleasure brings gentle, life-giving moisture like dawn dew.

18:12.rendering: A foolish son is ruin to his father, and a wife's quarreling is a constant dripping of water.
18:12.note: The Hebrew pairs the son's folly with the wife's contentiousness using vivid household imagery, where her endless bickering echoes like water relentlessly dripping through a roof.

18:13.rendering: A house and wealth are inherited from fathers, but a prudent wife is from the Lord.
18:13.note: The proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "prudent" (sakal), the same root used for the wise manager in Joseph's story and the insightful woman of Proverbs 31.

18:14.rendering: Slothfulness casts a person into a deep sleep, and an idle soul will go hungry.
18:14.note: The Hebrew pairs the sluggard's deep slumber with the literal starvation of his appetite, showing how laziness feeds on itself in a self-fulfilling cycle.

18:15.rendering: Whoever guards instruction guards his own life, but whoever despises his ways will die.
18:15.note: The Hebrew word for "guards" echoes the cherubim who "guard" the way to the tree of life in Genesis 3:24, turning wisdom into a matter of life and death.

18:16.rendering: Whoever is gracious to the poor lends to the LORD, and he will repay him for his good deed.
18:16.note: The Hebrew verb for "lend" here echoes the language of loans in Deuteronomy, turning charity into an investment with God himself as the borrower.

18:17.rendering: Discipline your son while there is still hope, but do not set your heart on putting him to death.
18:17.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "discipline" (yassar) and "set your heart" (al-tissa) warns that unchecked anger in correction can cross into destructive rage, an ancient wisdom call to measured parenting.

18:18.rendering: A hot-tempered person pays the penalty; if you rescue him, you will only have to do it again.
18:18.note: The Hebrew word for "rescue" echoes the same verb used for delivering someone from danger, hinting that enabling rage simply resets the cycle.

18:19.rendering: Listen to advice and accept instruction, so that in the end you may be wise.
18:19.note: This proverb echoes the book's opening call to heed wisdom's voice, forming character through repeated listening rather than isolated insight.

18:20.rendering: Many plans fill a person's heart, but the counsel of the LORD, that will stand.
18:20.note: The Hebrew word for "counsel" echoes the same term used for God's creative decree in Genesis 1, showing his single purpose outlasts all our scattered schemes.

18:21.rendering: What is desirable in a person is their loyalty, and a poor person is better than a liar.
18:21.note: The Hebrew word for "loyalty" here is hesed, the same covenant-love that binds God to his people.

18:22.rendering: The fear of the LORD leads to life, and one rests satisfied, untouched by harm.
18:22.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "fear" (yir'ah) that also carries the sense of awe-filled reverence, promising both vitality and a deep, unharmed contentment.

18:23.rendering: The sluggard buries his hand in the dish, and will not even bring it back to his mouth.
18:23.note: This vivid Hebrew hyperbole paints the lazy person as so inert he cannot finish the simplest act of self-care, echoing the book's repeated warning that sloth leads to self-destruction.

19:0.rendering: Wine is a mocker, strong drink a brawler, and whoever is led astray by it is not wise.
19:0.note: The Hebrew word for "led astray" echoes the wayward path of the fool in Proverbs 1-9, painting alcohol as a seductive companion that mocks those who follow it.

19:1.rendering: The terror of a king is like the roar of a lion, and whoever provokes him forfeits his own life.
19:1.note: The Hebrew word for "terror" echoes the dread of a lion's roar in the wild, a vivid image of royal power that demands respect or risks death.

19:2.rendering: It is an honor for a person to stay out of strife, but every fool jumps right into a fight.
19:2.note: The Hebrew word for "fool" here (kəsîl) pictures someone thick-headed and quarrel-loving, contrasting sharply with the wise who choose de-escalation as true strength.

19:3.rendering: The lazy person does not plow in autumn, so at harvest time he looks but there is nothing.
19:3.note: This proverb uses the farmer's calendar to expose how poor choices today quietly close doors tomorrow.

19:4.rendering: Deep waters are the plans in a person's heart, but a person of understanding will draw them out.
19:4.note: The Hebrew word for "draw out" is the same one used for drawing water from a well, picturing skillful insight as the rope and bucket that brings hidden motives to light.

19:5.rendering: Many a person proclaims their own loyal love, but a faithful person, who can find?
19:5.note: The Hebrew word for "faithful" here echoes the covenant term hesed, making this a search for someone whose loyal love actually matches their claims.

19:6.rendering: A righteous person walks in integrity, and his children are blessed after him.
19:6.note: The Hebrew word for "integrity" (tom) echoes the blameless walk of Noah and Job, painting the parent as a path-maker whose steps shape the next generation's inheritance.

19:7.rendering: A king seated on the throne of judgment winnows all evil with his eyes.
19:7.note: The Hebrew word for "winnows" echoes the image of a farmer tossing grain to separate wheat from chaff, revealing how a wise ruler discerns hearts at a glance.

18:24.rendering: Strike a mocker, and the naive becomes shrewd. Rebuke one who has understanding, and he gains knowledge.
18:24.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "mocker" (letz) and "shrewd" (ya'arim) highlights how correction ripples outward: one fool's punishment sharpens the simple, while the wise actually grow from direct rebuke.

18:25.rendering: Whoever mistreats his father and drives out his mother is a shameful and disgraceful child.
18:25.note: The Hebrew word for "shameful" here echoes the name of the fool's son in Proverbs 10:5, linking family betrayal to the deeper folly that unravels a household.

18:26.rendering: Stop listening to instruction, my son, and you will stray from words of knowledge.
18:26.note: The Hebrew verb for "stray" echoes the same root used for the rebellious son who wanders from wisdom's path in earlier proverbs, showing how rejecting discipline is the first step down a well-worn road of folly.

18:27.rendering: A worthless witness mocks at justice, and the mouth of the wicked swallows iniquity.
18:27.note: The Hebrew pairs "worthless witness" (beliyya'al) with "swallows" (y'bal-la), echoing the devouring imagery of the worthless man who gulps down evil like food.

18:28.rendering: Judgments are prepared for scoffers, and blows for the back of fools.
18:28.note: The Hebrew pairs "judgments" (mishpatim) with scoffers and "blows" (mahalumot) with fools, echoing the rod of discipline in Proverbs 13:24 and 22:15.

19:8.rendering: Who can say, "I have made my heart clean, I am pure from my sin"?
19:8.note: This rhetorical question echoes the ancient wisdom tradition's realism about human sinfulness, much like the psalmist's cry in Psalm 51.

19:9.rendering: Differing weights and differing measures, both of them are an abomination to the Lord.
19:9.note: The Hebrew word for "abomination" echoes the language used for unclean food and idols, revealing how everyday business fraud was as repulsive to God as idolatry.

19:10.rendering: Even a child makes himself known by his deeds, whether his conduct is pure and whether it is upright.
19:10.note: The Hebrew word for "makes himself known" echoes how God reveals his own character through actions, inviting us to watch small patterns that disclose the heart long before adulthood.

19:11.rendering: The ear that hears and the eye that sees, the LORD has made them both.
19:11.note: This proverb echoes the creation motif of Genesis, reminding us that our senses are deliberate gifts from the Creator, not random accidents.

19:12.rendering: Love sleep and you will end up poor, open your eyes and you will have bread to eat.
19:12.note: This proverb uses a sharp wordplay on "love sleep" (aheb shenah) versus "open your eyes," echoing the earlier call in Proverbs to pursue wisdom with eager alertness.

19:13.rendering: "Bad, bad," says the buyer, but when he walks away, then he boasts.
19:13.note: The Hebrew doubles the word for "bad" to capture the buyer's exaggerated haggling, a vivid window into ancient marketplace psychology.

19:14.rendering: There is gold and a multitude of rubies, but the lips of knowledge are a precious vessel.
19:14.note: This proverb plays on the image of a rare, finely wrought container that outshines raw treasure, elevating wise speech above mere wealth.

19:15.rendering: Take the garment of one who has gone surety for a stranger, and hold it in pledge when he has pledged for foreigners.
19:15.note: This proverb plays on the ancient practice of seizing collateral, urging shrewd protection against risky loans to outsiders whose reliability is unknown.

14:0.rendering: A gentle answer turns away rage, but a harsh word stirs up anger.
14:0.note: The Hebrew pairs "soft answer" with "turning away fury" like a physical deflection, showing how words shape conflict in ancient wisdom.

14:1.rendering: The tongue of the wise makes knowledge attractive, but the mouth of fools pours out folly.
14:1.note: The Hebrew word for "attractive" carries the idea of healing or making something pleasant, contrasting the wise tongue's skillful curation of knowledge with the fool's careless flood of nonsense.

14:2.rendering: The eyes of the LORD are everywhere, keeping watch on the evil and the good.
14:2.note: This echoes the ancient Near Eastern idea of a king's all-seeing gaze, but here it belongs to Yahweh, who watches both wicked and righteous without favoritism.

14:3.rendering: A healing tongue is a tree of life, but crooked speech breaks the spirit.
14:3.note: The Hebrew word for "crooked" speech is the same one used for "perverse" or "twisted" in Proverbs 2:15, painting a vivid contrast between words that mend like a life-giving tree and words that shatter like a breaking bone.

14:4.rendering: A fool spurns his father's instruction, but whoever heeds correction shows prudence.
14:4.note: The Hebrew word for "correction" here is the same one used for the "discipline" that builds wisdom throughout Proverbs, turning a fool's rejection into a path toward shrewd insight.

14:5.rendering: In the house of the righteous there is great treasure, but the revenue of the wicked brings trouble.
14:5.note: The Hebrew word for "treasure" here echoes the same term used for Joseph's stored grain in Egypt, hinting that true abundance comes from wisdom-aligned living, not just shrewd profit.

14:6.rendering: The lips of the wise spread knowledge, but the heart of fools is not so.
14:6.note: In Hebrew the wise "scatter" knowledge like seed on good soil, while the fool's heart stays closed and unfruitful, an image that echoes the parable of the sower centuries later.

14:7.rendering: The sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination to the LORD, but the prayer of the upright is his delight.
14:7.note: This verse echoes the prophets' critique of empty ritual: what matters is the heart behind the offering, not the offering itself.

19:16.rendering: Bread gained by deceit is sweet to a man, but afterward his mouth will be filled with gravel.
19:16.note: The Hebrew word for "gravel" (ḥāṣāṣ) echoes the gritty pain of betrayal, turning stolen pleasure into literal discomfort in your mouth.

19:17.rendering: Plans are established by counsel, and by wise guidance wage war.
19:17.note: The Hebrew pairs "counsel" with the same root used for a ship's rigging, picturing strategy as the ropes that steady a campaign like a vessel in storm.

19:18.rendering: A gossip goes about revealing secrets, so do not associate with one who opens his lips too wide.
19:18.note: The Hebrew pairs the gossip's footloose wandering with his loose lips, a vivid wordplay that warns character is contagious.

19:19.rendering: Whoever curses his father or his mother, his lamp will go out in the blackest darkness.
19:19.note: The Hebrew pairs "curse" with a lamp snuffed into "outer darkness," echoing the fifth commandment's promise of long life in the land.

19:20.rendering: An inheritance gained hastily in the beginning will not be blessed in the end.
19:20.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "hastily" (mebohal) and "blessed" (teborak) underscores how rushed gain unravels over time, echoing the wisdom theme that true inheritance comes through patient character, not shortcuts.

19:21.rendering: Do not say, "I will repay evil." Wait for the LORD, and he will deliver you.
19:21.note: This proverb echoes the ancient wisdom of leaving vengeance to God, a thread that runs from Deuteronomy 32:35 into Romans 12:19.

19:22.rendering: Yahweh detests differing weights, and dishonest scales are not good.
19:22.note: The Hebrew word for "detest" (to'evah) is the same one used for idolatry and sexual perversion in the Torah, revealing how God views economic deceit as covenant-breaking rebellion.

19:23.rendering: A person's steps are from the Lord, how then can anyone understand their own way?
19:23.note: The Hebrew word for "understand" here echoes the wisdom motif of discernment, yet this proverb humbly admits even the wise cannot fully trace their path apart from God's sovereign ordering.

14:8.rendering: The way of the wicked is an abomination to the LORD, but he loves the one who pursues righteousness.
14:8.note: The Hebrew pairs "abomination" with "love," creating a stark emotional contrast that echoes the wisdom theme of God's personal delight in the chase after justice.

14:9.rendering: Stern discipline awaits the one who abandons the path; whoever hates correction will die.
14:9.note: The Hebrew word for "correction" here is the same one used for the "discipline" a father gives a son in Proverbs 3:11-12, linking personal rebellion to the deeper wisdom journey that later echoes in Hebrews 12.

14:10.rendering: The grave and destruction lie open before the LORD, how much more the hearts of human beings.
14:10.note: Sheol and Abaddon, the most hidden realms of the dead, lie naked before God, this proverb shocks us by saying our own hearts are even more exposed to his gaze.

14:11.rendering: A scoffer does not love the one who corrects him, nor will he go to the wise.
14:11.note: The scoffer and the wise form a classic Proverbs pair, this time showing the scoffer’s allergy to any voice that might shrink his ego.

14:12.rendering: A glad heart makes the face shine with joy, but heartache breaks the spirit.
14:12.note: The Hebrew word for "heart" (lev) links this to the inner core of a person, while the rare term for "breaks the spirit" echoes the shattering of a vessel in ancient pottery imagery.

14:13.rendering: The heart of the discerning seeks knowledge, but the mouth of fools feeds on folly.
14:13.note: The Hebrew pairs "heart seeks" with "mouth grazes," painting fools as animals contentedly chewing on empty nonsense.

14:14.rendering: All the days of the afflicted are evil, but a cheerful heart has a continual feast.
14:14.note: The Hebrew word for "afflicted" (ani) echoes the poor and oppressed throughout Proverbs, contrasting their grinding anxiety with the inner resilience that turns ordinary days into a banquet.

14:15.rendering: Better a little with the fear of the LORD than great treasure and turmoil with it.
14:15.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "turmoil" (mehumah), which echoes the chaotic panic of Egypt's plagues, reminding us that wealth without reverence invites inner Egypt-like disorder.

19:24.rendering: It is a trap for a man to rashly say, "It is holy!" and only after the vow to consider his promise.
19:24.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "trap" (moqesh) and "vow" highlights how hasty dedication of something to God can snare you, a wisdom warning echoed in Ecclesiastes 5:4-5.

19:25.rendering: A wise king scatters the wicked, and he rolls a threshing wheel over them.
19:25.note: The Hebrew verb for "scatters" is the same one used for winnowing grain, so the wise king both separates evildoers and crushes them like a farmer driving the threshing sledge.

19:26.rendering: The breath of a human is the lamp of Yahweh, searching all the innermost chambers of the belly.
19:26.note: In Hebrew, neshamah (breath) echoes God's life-giving breath in Genesis 2:7, turning every person into a living lamp that Yahweh uses to illumine the hidden corners of the heart.

19:27.rendering: Loyal love and faithfulness watch over the king, and his throne is upheld by loyalty.
19:27.note: The Hebrew pairs "chesed" (loyal love) and "emet" (faithfulness), the very words God uses to describe himself in Exodus 34:6, now guarding the king like divine bodyguards.

19:28.rendering: The glory of young men is their strength, but the splendor of old men is their gray hair.
19:28.note: In the ancient world, gray hair signaled not frailty but the hard-won wisdom that comes only with decades of lived experience.

19:29.rendering: Blows that wound cleanse away evil, and strokes reach the innermost parts.
19:29.note: The Hebrew pairs "blows that wound" with a rare verb for deep cleansing, echoing how physical discipline in ancient wisdom was seen to scour the hidden chambers of the heart like a medical purge.

6:0.rendering: My son, guard my words, and treasure my commands within you.
6:0.note: The Hebrew pairs "guard" (shamar) with "treasure" (tsafan), echoing how Israel stored manna and the ark, turning parental wisdom into sacred, protected space.

6:1.rendering: Keep my commands and live, guard my teaching as the apple of your eye.
6:1.note: The Hebrew literally says "pupil of your eye," evoking the most vulnerable and protected spot in the body, an image that echoes Deuteronomy 32:10 where God guards Israel the same way.

6:2.rendering: Tie them on your fingers, write them on the tablet of your heart.
6:2.note: This echoes Deuteronomy 6:8 and 11:18, where God's words are to be bound as a sign on the hand and inscribed on the heart, transforming external law into internal wisdom.

6:3.rendering: Say to wisdom, "You are my sister," and call understanding your close relative.
6:3.note: This personifies wisdom as family, echoing the intimate bond in Proverbs 8 where she stands beside God at creation.

6:4.rendering: so that she may guard you from the strange woman, from the outsider whose words are smooth.
6:4.note: The Hebrew word for "guard" echoes its use in Proverbs 2:11 where wisdom herself keeps watch, showing how the father's words become internalized protection.

6:5.rendering: For at the window of my house I looked out through my lattice.
6:5.note: The father peers through latticework, an ancient window screen that hid him while revealing the street scene below.

6:6.rendering: I saw among the naive ones, I noticed among the youths a young man lacking sense.
6:6.note: The Hebrew word for "lacking sense" literally means "empty of heart," painting the youth as hollow where wisdom should dwell.

6:7.rendering: He passes along the street near her corner, and he walks toward her house,
6:7.note: The Hebrew verb for "walks" carries the sense of straying or stepping off the path, quietly echoing the father’s earlier warnings about the crooked ways of the wicked.

7:0.rendering: Does not Wisdom call out? Does not Understanding lift her voice?
7:0.note: The Hebrew verbs here paint Wisdom as a bold woman shouting in the streets, an image that echoes Lady Folly's seductive calls later in the chapter.

7:1.rendering: She takes her stand on the high places beside the road, at the crossroads she stations herself.
7:1.note: The Hebrew word for "stations herself" echoes the same verb used when God "takes his stand" to confront Moses on Sinai, hinting that Lady Wisdom is no mere human teacher but a divine voice calling from the heights.

7:2.rendering: Beside the gates, at the entrance to the city, at the doors she cries aloud.
7:2.note: Wisdom stations herself at the bustling city gates where decisions are made, echoing how kings and judges once sat there to dispense justice.

7:3.rendering: To you, O people, I call out, and my voice is to the children of man.
7:3.note: Wisdom's public cry echoes Lady Folly's invitation in chapter 9, setting up a street-level choice between life and death.

7:4.rendering: You who are naive, learn shrewdness. You fools, get a heart that understands.
7:4.note: The Hebrew pairs "simple ones" with "prudence" and "fools" with "heart of understanding," showing wisdom can transform even the gullible and senseless.

7:5.rendering: Listen, for I speak noble things, and my lips open with what is right.
7:5.note: The Hebrew pairs "noble things" with a word for "princes" or "leaders," hinting that wisdom's words shape true rulers.

7:6.rendering: For my mouth will utter truth, and wickedness is an abomination to my lips.
7:6.note: The Hebrew pairs "truth" with "wickedness" as its direct opposite, showing wisdom trains the tongue to hate what distorts reality.

7:7.rendering: All the words of my mouth are righteous, none of them twisted or crooked.
7:7.note: The Hebrew word for "crooked" (niftal) literally means "twisted," echoing the serpent's crafty path in Genesis 3.

5:0.rendering: My son, if you have become a guarantor for your neighbor, if you have shaken hands in pledge for a stranger,
5:0.note: The Hebrew word for "struck hands" pictures a binding, physical act of solidarity that echoes the ancient practice of sealing risky alliances with a handshake.

5:1.rendering: you are snared by the words of your mouth, caught by the sayings of your lips.
5:1.note: The Hebrew word for "snared" (yāqaʿ) evokes a hunter's trap, turning your own casual words into the mechanism that captures you.

5:2.rendering: So do this, my son, and rescue yourself, since you have come into the hand of your neighbor: go, humble yourself, plead urgently with your neighbor.
5:2.note: The urgent command to "humble yourself" (literally "trample your own honor") flips the ancient shame code, showing wisdom demands swallowing pride to escape a self-made trap.

5:3.rendering: Give no sleep to your eyes, and no slumber to your eyelids.
5:3.note: This urgent command echoes the father's earlier call in Proverbs 4:25 to fix your gaze straight ahead, forming a pair of eyes-and-ears instructions that train single-minded wisdom.

5:4.rendering: Free yourself like a gazelle from the hunter's hand, like a bird from the fowler's snare.
5:4.note: The Hebrew wordplay pits 'hand' (yad) against 'snare' (yad), turning the trap itself into the thing you must escape like a wild animal.

5:5.rendering: Go to the ant, you sluggard. Observe her ways and become wise.
5:5.note: The Hebrew word for sluggard evokes someone who shrinks back from effort, while the ant's self-starting industry becomes a master class in wisdom without needing a boss.

5:6.rendering: Though it has no commander, officer, or ruler,
5:6.note: The ant's self-organized wisdom echoes the ancient Near Eastern ideal of order without coercion.

5:7.rendering: It prepares its food in summer, and gathers its sustenance in harvest.
5:7.note: The Hebrew word for "prepares" (takhin) echoes God's own provision in the wilderness, showing even the ant participates in divine wisdom's rhythm of work and trust.

14:16.rendering: Better a dinner of vegetables where love is, than a fattened ox and hatred with it.
14:16.note: The Hebrew wordplay pits "vegetables" (simple greens) against the prized "fattened ox," showing that love at the table transforms even poverty into abundance.

14:17.rendering: A hot-tempered person stirs up conflict, but one slow to anger calms a quarrel.
14:17.note: The Hebrew word for "slow to anger" echoes God's own self-description in Exodus 34:6, painting patient people as those who reflect divine character in everyday friction.

14:18.rendering: The way of a sluggard is like a hedge of thorns, but the path of the upright is a level highway.
14:18.note: The Hebrew word for “sluggard” (atsel) plays on laziness that turns life into a prickly, impassable thicket.

14:19.rendering: A wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish man despises his mother.
14:19.note: The Hebrew word for "despises" (bazah) is the same one used for Esau's contempt of his birthright, quietly linking family dishonor to covenant-breaking folly.

14:20.rendering: Folly brings joy to the one who lacks sense, but a person of understanding walks a straight path.
14:20.note: The Hebrew pairs "heart without sense" with "straight path," contrasting the fool's giddy detours with the wise person's clear direction.

14:21.rendering: Plans fail without consultation, but they succeed with many advisers.
14:21.note: The Hebrew word for "plans" (mahshabot) echoes God's own thoughts in Jeremiah 29:11, inviting us to see human strategy as a humble participation in divine wisdom.

14:22.rendering: A person finds joy in the right answer from their mouth, and how good is a word in its season.
14:22.note: The phrase "word in its season" echoes the rhythms of Ecclesiastes 3, training us to see timely speech as a form of wisdom that brings communal delight.

14:23.rendering: The path of life leads upward for the insightful one, so he turns away from the grave below.
14:23.note: The Hebrew word for "path" echoes the "way" of wisdom in chapters 1-9, painting life as an uphill climb that wisely avoids Sheol's descent.

7:8.rendering: They are all straightforward to one who understands, and right to those who find knowledge.
7:8.note: The Hebrew word for "straightforward" (nakoach) carries the sense of something level and smooth, like a cleared path, echoing the call in verse 1-2 to walk wisdom's straight roads rather than crooked ones.

7:9.rendering: Take my instruction instead of silver, and knowledge rather than choice gold.
7:9.note: Wisdom personified invites a trade: her teaching over the ancient world's most precious metals.

7:10.rendering: For wisdom is better than jewels, and all desirable things cannot compare with her.
7:10.note: The Hebrew word for "jewels" here echoes the high priest's breastplate gems in Exodus 28, positioning wisdom as surpassing even the most sacred treasures of Israel.

7:11.rendering: I, Wisdom, dwell with shrewdness, and I find knowledge with discretion.
7:11.note: The Hebrew verb for "dwell" is the same one used for God tabernacling among his people, hinting that wisdom is not abstract but a personal presence to live with.

7:12.rendering: To fear the LORD is to hate evil. I hate pride and arrogance, the way of evil and perverse speech.
7:12.note: The Hebrew links "fear of the LORD" directly to hating what God hates, creating an ancient wisdom equation: reverence equals moral disgust at hubris.

7:13.rendering: Counsel is mine, and sound wisdom. I am understanding. Strength belongs to me.
7:13.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" here is the same one used for God's power in creation and for the king's might, linking Lady Wisdom directly to divine authority.

7:14.rendering: By me kings reign, and rulers decree what is just.
7:14.note: The Hebrew verb for "decree" echoes the word for "engrave," portraying wise rule as something chiseled into reality like ancient law on stone.

7:15.rendering: By me kings reign, and rulers decree what is just.
7:15.note: The Hebrew word for "decree" (yachok) echoes the "statute" (choq) given at Sinai, portraying Woman Wisdom as the living source behind just governance.

6:8.rendering: in the twilight, as the day was fading, at the approach of night and darkness.
6:8.note: The Hebrew piles up time-words like a gathering shadow: twilight, evening gloom, deep night, revealing the slow, deliberate slide into moral danger.

6:9.rendering: Then a woman comes out to meet him, dressed like a prostitute, with a hidden heart.
6:9.note: The Hebrew literally says she has a guarded or cunning heart, contrasting her loud public dress with her secret schemes.

6:10.rendering: She is loud and defiant, her feet do not stay at home.
6:10.note: The Hebrew pairs "loud" (noisy, boisterous) with "defiant" (stubbornly rebellious), painting her as the exact opposite of the wise woman whose household is ordered and secure.

6:11.rendering: In the street she lurks, in the squares she lies in wait, at every corner she watches.
6:11.note: The Hebrew verbs paint her as an active predator who "lies in wait" (the same word used for wild animals ambushing prey in Job 38:40 and Psalm 10:9).

6:12.rendering: She grabs him and kisses him, and with a bold face she says to him,
6:12.note: The Hebrew verb for "grabs" is the same one used when Potiphar's wife seizes Joseph, flipping the aggressive seductress script.

6:13.rendering: I had to make fellowship offerings today, so now I have meat from the sacrifice at home.
6:13.note: The woman cleverly repurposes sacred peace-offering meat (which had to be eaten promptly) as bait for her seduction, blending piety with predation.

6:14.rendering: So I have come out to meet you, to seek your face, and I have found you.
6:14.note: The Hebrew word for "seek your face" echoes the same phrase used for seeking God's presence in the temple.

6:15.rendering: I have spread my bed with coverings, colored cloths of Egyptian linen.
6:15.note: The Hebrew for "colored cloths" (literally "thread of Egypt") winks at the seductive power of exotic luxury, echoing how the strange woman lures with the very treasures God once gave Israel from Egypt.

13:0.rendering: The wise woman builds her house, but the foolish one tears it down with her own hands.
13:0.note: The Hebrew word for "builds" echoes God's own creative work in Genesis, inviting women into the divine pattern of constructing life and community rather than dismantling it.

13:1.rendering: Whoever walks in his integrity fears the LORD, but the one who is crooked in his ways despises him.
13:1.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "crooked ways" with "despising" God, showing that a twisted life path is itself an act of contempt toward the one who made straight paths.

13:2.rendering: In the mouth of a fool is a rod of pride, but the lips of the wise will preserve them.
13:2.note: The Hebrew word for "rod" (shebet) evokes both arrogant speech and the instrument of discipline, hinting that a fool's own words end up beating him.

13:3.rendering: Where there are no oxen the manger is clean, but abundant crops come by the strength of the ox.
13:3.note: The proverb plays on the mess of real work: clean stalls mean no harvest, while strong oxen bring both manure and bounty.

13:4.rendering: A trustworthy witness does not lie, but a false witness pours out lies.
13:4.note: The Hebrew verb for the false witness literally means "breathes out" lies, like a dragon spewing fire, contrasting the steady reliability of the truthful one.

13:5.rendering: A mocker seeks wisdom and finds none, but knowledge comes easily to the discerning.
13:5.note: The Hebrew word for "mocker" (lets) carries the idea of someone who deliberately scoffs at correction, making the verse a study in how posture shapes the ability to learn.

13:6.rendering: Go away from the presence of a foolish man, for you will not find lips of knowledge there.
13:6.note: The Hebrew pairs "fool" with the exact phrase "lips of knowledge" used in 10:32 and 15:7, showing wisdom is caught by whose company you keep.

13:7.rendering: The wisdom of the shrewd is to understand his way, but the folly of fools is deception.
13:7.note: The Hebrew word for "understand" here is the same one used for discerning the path of wisdom elsewhere in Proverbs, contrasting the shrewd who map their own road against fools who wander lost in self-deception.

14:24.rendering: The Lord tears down the house of the proud, but he sets the boundary stone of the widow.
14:24.note: Ancient boundary stones protected the vulnerable; God himself defends the widow's land against the greedy proud.

14:25.rendering: The LORD detests the schemes of the wicked, but pleasant words are pure in his sight.
14:25.note: The Hebrew pairs "thoughts/plans" of the wicked with "words" that are clean, showing how inner schemes surface in speech that either repels or delights God.

14:26.rendering: Whoever gets greedy for profit troubles his own house, but whoever hates bribes will live.
14:26.note: The Hebrew word for "profit" (betsa) often carries the dark sense of ill-gotten gain, echoing the same term used for Achan's covetous plunder in Joshua 7 that literally "troubled" Israel.

14:27.rendering: The heart of the righteous ponders how to answer, but the mouth of the wicked gushes out evil.
14:27.note: This proverb contrasts deep, measured thought (the Hebrew word "yehgeh" evokes a lion's low growl or careful meditation) with the wicked's hasty verbal flood.

14:28.rendering: The LORD is far from the wicked, but he hears the prayer of the righteous.
14:28.note: The word "far" echoes the ancient idea of God's presence as nearness; this proverb invites us to see prayer as drawing God close through righteous living.

14:29.rendering: Bright eyes gladden the heart, good news fattens the bones.
14:29.note: The Hebrew pairs "light of the eyes" with "good news" that literally makes bones thrive, an image of deep, embodied joy echoing how wisdom literature sees the body as a barometer of the heart.

14:30.rendering: The ear that listens to life-giving correction will lodge among the wise.
14:30.note: The Hebrew pairs "listens" with the same root as "ear," creating a wordplay that pictures the attentive ear finding a permanent home with the wise.

14:31.rendering: Whoever ignores instruction despises his own life, but whoever listens to reproof acquires understanding.
14:31.note: The Hebrew word for "despises" (maw'as) carries the force of actively rejecting or treating with contempt, making this a stark warning that refusing correction is self-sabotage.

7:16.rendering: I love those who love me, and those who seek me find me.
7:16.note: The Hebrew verb for "seek" (shachar) means to seek early, like at dawn, echoing the eager pursuit in Psalm 63:1 and Hosea 5:15.

7:17.rendering: Riches and honor are with me, enduring wealth and righteousness.
7:17.note: Wisdom personified here echoes the king's treasures in 1 Kings 3:13, where God grants Solomon riches and honor for choosing her first.

7:18.rendering: My fruit is better than gold, even fine gold, and my yield than choice silver.
7:18.note: The Hebrew word for "fruit" (peri) echoes the garden's life-giving produce, positioning Lady Wisdom as the true tree of life whose harvest outshines every earthly treasure.

7:19.rendering: I walk along the path of righteousness, in the midst of the tracks of justice.
7:19.note: The Hebrew for "walk" echoes the same word used for how the wise "go" through life, painting Lady Wisdom as the living embodiment of the two-track path of Proverbs.

7:20.rendering: I will fill their treasuries with wealth, and their storehouses with riches, so that those who love me inherit substance, and I will fill their treasuries.
7:20.note: The Hebrew word for "substance" (yesh) echoes God's self-revelation as "I AM" (Exodus 3:14), hinting that wisdom's lovers receive true being and fullness.

7:21.rendering: Yahweh created me at the beginning of his way, the first of his works from long ago.
7:21.note: The Hebrew qanah here means "acquired" or "created," echoing Genesis 14:19 where God is maker of heaven and earth.

7:22.rendering: I was set up from everlasting, from the beginning, before the earth began.
7:22.note: The Hebrew word for "set up" (nissakti) carries the image of being poured out or installed like a king at his coronation, hinting at wisdom's royal authority from before creation.

7:23.rendering: When there were no deeps, I was brought forth, when there were no springs abounding with water.
7:23.note: The Hebrew "brought forth" echoes a woman in labor, personifying Wisdom as birthed before the chaotic waters of creation.

6:16.rendering: I have perfumed my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.
6:16.note: The three exotic spices echo the sacred anointing oil of the tabernacle, turning a seduction scene into a twisted echo of holy worship.

6:17.rendering: Come, let us drink our fill of love until morning, let us delight ourselves with caresses.
6:17.note: The Hebrew verb for "drink our fill" echoes the intoxicating language of the Song of Songs, but here it's twisted into the adulteress's seductive trap.

6:18.rendering: For the man is not at home, he has gone on a long journey.
6:18.note: The Hebrew word for "long journey" carries the sense of a distant, open-ended trip, heightening the adulteress's calculated timing.

6:19.rendering: He has taken the money bag in his hand. He will come home at the full moon.
6:19.note: The Hebrew "bag of the silver" echoes the very phrase used for Joseph's brothers' silver in Genesis 42, hinting at hidden danger behind everyday commerce.

6:20.rendering: She sways him with her many persuasions, with her smooth lips she compels him.
6:20.note: The Hebrew verb for "compels" is the same one used for how the serpent beguiled Eve, quietly echoing an older story of deceptive speech leading to ruin.

6:21.rendering: He follows her at once, like an ox going to the slaughter, or like a fool stumbling to the stocks for correction.
6:21.note: The Hebrew wordplay links the young man to a sacrificial animal and a shackled prisoner, both marching toward death without realizing it.

6:22.rendering: until an arrow pierces his liver, like a bird rushing into a snare, unaware that it will cost him his life.
6:22.note: The liver here is the seat of life in ancient thought, and the snare echoes the bird-trap imagery of Ecclesiastes 9:12, where sudden death catches the unwary.

6:23.rendering: And now, sons, listen to me, pay attention to the words of my mouth.
6:23.note: The father repeats his urgent call to "sons" exactly as in 5:7 and 4:1, weaving this warning into a tapestry of fatherly instruction.

13:8.rendering: Fools mock at sin's guilt offering, but the upright find favor among the blameless.
13:8.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "mock" (yālîṣ) with "guilt offering" (āšām), exposing how the fool treats atonement itself as a joke while the upright enjoy acceptance.

13:9.rendering: The heart knows its own bitterness, and no stranger can share in its joy.
13:9.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "heart" (leb) as the innermost self, inaccessible to outsiders, even in both grief and celebration.

13:10.rendering: The house of the wicked will be destroyed, but the tent of the upright will flourish.
13:10.note: The Hebrew wordplay contrasts the solid "house" (bayit) of the wicked with the flimsy "tent" (ohel) of the upright, yet it is the fragile tent that endures and blossoms.

13:11.rendering: There is a way that seems right to a person, but its end is the ways of death.
13:11.note: The Hebrew word for "way" (derek) echoes throughout Proverbs like a path you choose, but this proverb warns that self-deception can make a dead-end road feel like the right one.

13:12.rendering: Even in laughter the heart can ache, and the end of joy may be grief.
13:12.note: The Hebrew word for "end" (acharit) echoes the book's wisdom on final outcomes, showing how surface joy often masks deeper sorrow.

13:13.rendering: The faithless will be filled with the fruit of their own ways, but the good person will be satisfied from what is in them.
13:13.note: The Hebrew word for "filled" echoes the garden's tree of knowledge, where choosing one's own way produced its own bitter harvest.

13:14.rendering: The naive believes every word, but the shrewd understands where he sets his foot.
13:14.note: The Hebrew pairs the gullible "simple one" with the wise who "discerns his step," echoing the path imagery that runs through Proverbs like a trail of wisdom or folly.

13:15.rendering: A wise person fears and turns away from evil, but a fool is reckless and gets angry.
13:15.note: The Hebrew word for "reckless" (boteach) plays on the idea of false confidence, contrasting the wise who actively "fear and turn" with the fool who barrels ahead in misplaced trust.

14:32.rendering: The fear of the LORD is the discipline of wisdom, and humility comes before honor.
14:32.note: This pairs "fear of Yahweh" with "discipline of wisdom," echoing the book's opening (1:7) while showing humility as the surprising path to the honor the proverbs prize.

7:24.rendering: Before the mountains were sunk, before the hills I was brought forth,
7:24.note: The Hebrew verb for "brought forth" is the same one used for a woman giving birth, painting Wisdom as a cherished firstborn before creation itself.

7:25.rendering: before he made the earth and the open fields, or the first dust of the world.
7:25.note: The Hebrew "dust" (aphar) echoes Genesis 2:7, where God forms humanity from the ground, hinting at wisdom's role even in our origins.

7:26.rendering: When he established the heavens, I was there, when he drew a circle on the face of the deep,
7:26.note: The Hebrew verb for "drew a circle" evokes the ancient image of God using a compass to mark the horizon where sea meets sky, portraying Wisdom as eyewitness to the very act of cosmic boundary-setting.

7:27.rendering: When he made the skies firm above, when he established the fountains of the deep,
7:27.note: The Hebrew pairs the sky's vault with the deep's fountains, echoing Genesis 1's separation of waters above and below.

7:28.rendering: when he set the limit for the sea, that the waters should not overflow his command, when he marked out the foundations of the earth,
7:28.note: The word for "limit" here is the same as the boundary God sets for the chaotic sea in Job 38:10, portraying wisdom as present at creation's first victory over disorder.

7:29.rendering: I was beside him as a master craftsman, and I was daily his delight, rejoicing before him at all times.
7:29.note: The Hebrew "amon" carries the double sense of skilled artisan and trusted nursling, painting Wisdom as both God's master builder and his cherished child.

7:30.rendering: rejoicing in his inhabited world, and my delight was with the children of humanity.
7:30.note: The Hebrew for "delight" here is the same word used for God's pleasure in his beloved Son at Jesus' baptism, hinting that Wisdom's joy in humanity foreshadows the Trinity's eternal delight in us.

7:31.rendering: So now, children, listen to me: happy are those who keep my ways.
7:31.note: The Hebrew word for "happy" (ashre) is the same one that opens the entire book in 1:1 and 3:13, echoing the blessing of those who embrace Lady Wisdom's paths.

6:24.rendering: Do not let your heart turn aside to her ways, do not stray into her paths.
6:24.note: The Hebrew verb for "turn aside" echoes the same word used for Israel's repeated straying after idols throughout the prophets.

6:25.rendering: For she has brought down many wounded, and a mighty host are all those she has slain.
6:25.note: The Hebrew verb “cast down” echoes the battlefield language of military defeat, turning the seductive woman into a ruthless warrior whose victims pile up like fallen soldiers.

6:26.rendering: Her house is the highway to Sheol, descending to the chambers of death.
6:26.note: The Hebrew word for "chambers" echoes the inner sanctuary of the temple, twisting sacred space into a tomb.

13:16.rendering: A quick-tempered person acts foolishly, but one who schemes patiently is hated.
13:16.note: The Hebrew word for "quick-tempered" literally means "short of nose," contrasting the hot, hasty fool with the cold, calculating schemer who ends up despised.

13:17.rendering: The naive inherit foolishness, but the shrewd wear knowledge as a crown.
13:17.note: The Hebrew wordplay pits the "simple" (pethi) who end up owning folly against the "shrewd" (arum) who are crowned with knowledge, echoing the serpent's cunning in Genesis 3:1.

13:18.rendering: The evil bow down before the good, and the wicked at the gates of the righteous.
13:18.note: This proverb pictures a dramatic reversal where the wicked must humble themselves at the city gates, the ancient place of justice and honor, echoing how wisdom literature often shows evil ultimately yielding to good.

13:19.rendering: The poor are hated even by their neighbors, but the rich have many friends.
13:19.note: This proverb uses stark parallelism to expose how wealth warps human relationships in ancient village life.

13:20.rendering: Whoever despises his neighbor is a sinner, but whoever shows favor to the poor is blessed.
13:20.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "despises" (baz) with "sinner" (hote), contrasting it with the blessedness of kindness to the lowly.

13:21.rendering: Do not those who plot evil wander astray? But kindness and faithfulness belong to those who plan good.
13:21.note: The Hebrew word for "plan" or "plot" is the same root used in Genesis 50:20 where Joseph says his brothers "planned" evil but God "planned" it for good, showing how the proverb echoes that reversal of intent.

13:22.rendering: In all toil there is profit, but mere talk leads only to poverty.
13:22.note: The Hebrew word for "talk" here echoes the "lips" of the lazy in verse 15, exposing how empty words mimic the path to ruin.

13:23.rendering: The crown of the wise is their wealth, but the folly of fools is only folly.
13:23.note: The Hebrew word for "crown" (atarah) echoes the royal imagery of Proverbs 1-9, contrasting true honor with the self-deception that leaves fools empty-handed.

7:32.rendering: Listen to my instruction and become wise, do not neglect it.
7:32.note: The Hebrew for "listen" (shama) echoes the great Shema of Deuteronomy 6:4, calling Israel to attentive obedience that now leads to wisdom's table.

7:33.rendering: Blessed is the one who listens to me, watching daily at my gates, waiting at my doorposts.
7:33.note: The Hebrew word for "watching" (shqad) echoes the eager vigilance of a guard or lover at the door, inviting us to treat wisdom like a daily rendezvous rather than a checklist.

7:34.rendering: For the one who finds me finds life, and obtains favor from the LORD.
7:34.note: The Hebrew for "finds" echoes the urgent search in verse 17, turning wisdom's pursuit into a life-or-death quest that yields the Lord's own delight.

7:35.rendering: But the one who misses me does violence to his own soul. All who hate me love death.
7:35.note: The Hebrew word for "misses" (hata) is the same one usually translated "sins," turning this into a wisdom play on how rejecting Lady Wisdom is self-inflicted harm.

13:24.rendering: A truthful witness rescues lives, but a deceitful one pours out lies.
13:24.note: The Hebrew word for "rescues" (natsal) echoes the Exodus deliverance, portraying the truthful witness as a liberator who snatches people from danger.

13:25.rendering: In the fear of the LORD is strong confidence, and his children will have a refuge.
13:25.note: The Hebrew word for "fear" here carries the awe-filled reverence that anchors a whole household, turning personal trust into a shelter for generations.

13:26.rendering: The fear of the Lord is a fountain of life, turning one aside from the snares of death.
13:26.note: This proverb echoes the tree of life in Eden and Proverbs 13:14, picturing reverent awe as a fresh spring that pulls you away from deadly traps.

13:27.rendering: A king's glory is in the multitude of people, but without people a ruler is ruined.
13:27.note: The Hebrew pairs kavod (glory, heavy honor) with me'at (few, diminished), creating a wordplay that shows a leader's weight depends on the weight of the community he carries.

13:28.rendering: Whoever is slow to anger has great understanding, but the quick-tempered exalts folly.
13:28.note: The Hebrew word for "slow to anger" is the same phrase used of God in Exodus 34:6, painting patient humans as bearing the Creator's own image.

13:29.rendering: A tranquil heart is life to the flesh, but envy is rot in the bones.
13:29.note: The Hebrew wordplay contrasts "healing" (marpe) with "rot" (reqav), showing envy as a slow decay eating away at your very core.

13:30.rendering: Whoever oppresses the poor insults his maker, but the one who is gracious to the needy honors him.
13:30.note: The word for "oppress" here echoes the exodus language of harsh forced labor in Egypt, while "gracious" is the same term used for God's own mercy.

13:31.rendering: The wicked is overthrown by his own evil, but the righteous finds refuge even in his death.
13:31.note: The Hebrew word for "refuge" (machseh) echoes the Psalms' frequent cry for shelter in God, hinting that the righteous person's security outlasts physical death itself.

13:32.rendering: Wisdom rests quietly in the heart of the discerning, but in the midst of fools it makes itself known.
13:32.note: The Hebrew word for "rests" carries the idea of settling or lodging, contrasting the quiet home wisdom finds in the wise with the noisy display it gets among fools.

13:33.rendering: Righteousness exalts a nation, but sin is a reproach to any people.
13:33.note: The Hebrew word for "reproach" carries the sense of something that brings public disgrace, echoing how Israel's story shows both covenant blessing and national shame.

13:34.rendering: A king delights in a servant who acts wisely, but his rage falls on one who brings shame.
13:34.note: The Hebrew word for "delight" (ratson) echoes God's pleasure in acceptable sacrifices, hinting that a wise servant becomes a pleasing offering to the king.

5:8.rendering: How long will you lie there, sluggard? When will you rise from your sleep?
5:8.note: The Hebrew word for "sluggard" (atsel) carries a sting of contempt, like calling someone a professional loafer who chooses sleep over diligence.

5:9.rendering: A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest,
5:9.note: The Hebrew word for "a little" (me'at) repeats three times like a drowsy echo, mimicking the very laziness it warns against.

5:10.rendering: and your poverty will come like a bandit, and your lack like an armed warrior.
5:10.note: The Hebrew word for "bandit" pictures a swift, unexpected raider, turning laziness into sudden, violent loss.

5:11.rendering: A scoundrel, a man of no good, walks with a crooked mouth.
5:11.note: The Hebrew pairs "worthless man" (beliyya'al) with "crooked mouth," painting him as chaos walking: his speech literally twists what is straight.

5:12.rendering: He winks with his eyes, signals with his feet, points with his fingers.
5:12.note: These three gestures form a single ancient portrait of the schemer whose body language betrays deceit long before his words do.

5:13.rendering: Crookedness is in his heart, he plots evil all the time, he sows discord.
5:13.note: The Hebrew word for "sows" (zara) evokes scattering seed, turning conflict into an agricultural image of deliberate cultivation.

5:14.rendering: Therefore his disaster will come suddenly, in an instant he will be broken, and there will be no healing.
5:14.note: The Hebrew pairs "suddenly" with "in an instant" to paint a picture of irreversible shattering, echoing the wisdom theme that folly's end is swift and final.

5:15.rendering: There are six things Yahweh hates, yes seven that are an abomination to his soul.
5:15.note: This numerical saying uses the x, x+1 pattern to signal a complete yet overflowing list, a common wisdom device in the ancient Near East.

5:16.rendering: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood,
5:16.note: The first three items in this catalog of seven abominations open with body parts, painting the whole person as a walking threat to community life.

5:17.rendering: a heart that hatches wicked schemes, feet that are swift to run into evil,
5:17.note: The Hebrew pairs this scheming heart with "feet swift to run to evil," echoing the very first line of the Psalter that warns against walking, standing, or sitting in the way of the wicked.

5:18.rendering: a lying witness who breathes out falsehoods, and one who sows conflict among brothers.
5:18.note: The Hebrew verb for "sows" (shalach) carries the image of scattering seed, turning family discord into something deliberately cultivated rather than accidental.

5:19.rendering: My son, keep your father's command, and do not forsake the teaching of your mother.
5:19.note: The Hebrew pairs "command" (mitzvah) with father and "teaching" (torah) with mother, elevating both parents as sources of authoritative wisdom in the ancient household.

5:20.rendering: Bind them on your heart always, tie them around your neck.
5:20.note: The verbs echo the Shema in Deuteronomy 6:6-8, turning parental wisdom into something worn like a permanent amulet over the heart and throat.

5:21.rendering: When you walk, she will guide you. When you lie down, she will watch over you. When you wake, she will speak with you.
5:21.note: The Hebrew verbs form a rhythmic triplet that echoes the Shema in Deuteronomy 6:7, weaving wisdom into every moment of daily life.

5:22.rendering: For the commandment is a lamp, and the teaching a light, and the reproofs of discipline are the way of life.
5:22.note: The Hebrew pairs "commandment" with "lamp" and "teaching" (torah) with "light," echoing the creation imagery of Genesis 1 where God's word brings order out of darkness.

5:23.rendering: to keep you from the evil woman, from the smooth tongue of the foreign woman.
5:23.note: The Hebrew pairs "evil woman" with "foreign woman," evoking the dangerous outsider in ancient wisdom who uses seductive speech to lure the naive.

5:24.rendering: Do not covet her beauty in your heart, and do not let her captivate you with her eyelids.
5:24.note: The Hebrew verb for "captivate" (literally "take") echoes the language of theft and conquest, portraying lust as a form of plunder that starts in the heart.

5:25.rendering: For a prostitute's fee is only a loaf of bread, but a married woman's hunt takes a man's very life.
5:25.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "bread" (lechem) and "life" (nephesh) contrasts cheap, immediate hunger with the costly hunt that devours a man's whole being.

5:26.rendering: Can a man carry fire in his lap and his clothes not be burned?
5:26.note: The Hebrew word for "fire" echoes the blaze of illicit passion in the surrounding verses, turning a simple proverb into a vivid warning about desire's inevitable cost.

5:27.rendering: Or can a man walk on hot coals and his feet not be scorched?
5:27.note: The Hebrew word for "coals" (gehalim) echoes the burning judgment imagery of Proverbs 6:27's fire, painting a picture of inevitable consequences through vivid, sensory wisdom language.

5:28.rendering: So it is with the one who sleeps with his neighbor's wife. No one who touches her will go unpunished.
5:28.note: The Hebrew verb for "touch" echoes the warning in Genesis 3:3 about the forbidden fruit, linking sexual trespass to the original boundary crossed in Eden.

5:29.rendering: People do not despise a thief if he steals to satisfy himself when he is hungry,
5:29.note: This proverb uses the common ancient Near Eastern scenario of a starving thief to set up a contrast with the far greater folly of adultery in the verses that follow.

5:30.rendering: Yet if he is caught, he will pay sevenfold, he will give over all the wealth of his house.
5:30.note: The "sevenfold" repayment echoes the ancient legal principle in Exodus 22, turning the thief's desperate act into a total loss of inheritance.

5:31.rendering: Whoever commits adultery with a woman lacks sense. He who does it destroys his own soul.
5:31.note: The Hebrew word for "lacks sense" is the same one used for the naive youth seduced by Lady Folly in chapter 7, linking personal ruin to a deeper pattern of ignoring wisdom's call.

5:32.rendering: He will get wounds and dishonor, and his disgrace will not be wiped away.
5:32.note: The Hebrew word for "wounds" (nega) is the same one used for the plague-strokes in the Exodus story, turning private sin into a public, lasting mark of shame.

5:33.rendering: Jealousy is a husband's raging fury, and he will show no mercy on the day of reckoning.
5:33.note: The Hebrew word for "jealousy" here is qinah, the same fierce term used for God's own passion for his people throughout the Old Testament.

5:34.rendering: He will not accept any ransom, he will not be appeased though you multiply the gifts.
5:34.note: The Hebrew word for "gifts" (shohad) is the same one used for bribes elsewhere in Proverbs, exposing how even lavish attempts at buying off an enraged husband are useless.

8:0.rendering: Wisdom has built her house, she has hewn out her seven pillars.
8:0.note: The seven pillars echo the temple's sacred architecture, portraying Wisdom as a master builder inviting people into her sturdy, ordered home.

8:1.rendering: She has slaughtered her animals, mixed her wine, and set her table.
8:1.note: The Hebrew for "mixed her wine" (māzəgâ yāyin) points to spiced, diluted wine for a feast, echoing the lavish hospitality of Wisdom's ancient Near Eastern banquet.

8:2.rendering: She has sent out her servant girls, she calls from the highest point of the city.
8:2.note: The Hebrew verb for "sent out" echoes God's own sending of Wisdom into the world, like a royal summons from the heights where only the divine dwells.

8:3.rendering: Whoever is naive, let him turn in here. To the one lacking sense she says,
8:3.note: The Hebrew word for "naive" (peti) pictures an open door, an easily swayed youth, not a fool but one still moldable.

8:4.rendering: Come, eat my bread, and drink the wine I have mixed.
8:4.note: The Hebrew for "mixed" echoes the preparation of a royal banquet, inviting the simple to feast at wisdom's table like honored guests.

8:5.rendering: Leave behind your naive ways and live, walk in the way of understanding.
8:5.note: The Hebrew pairs "leave" (azav) with "live" (chayah), echoing the garden choice between folly's death and wisdom's life-giving path.

8:6.rendering: Whoever corrects a scoffer gets himself shame, and whoever rebukes a wicked man gets himself a blemish.
8:6.note: The Hebrew word for "blemish" echoes the language of ritual defilement in Leviticus, showing that trying to straighten out the unteachable can actually stain the wise person's own reputation.

8:7.rendering: Do not correct a scoffer, or he will hate you. Correct a wise person, and he will love you.
8:7.note: The Hebrew word for "correct" (yakach) echoes its use in Proverbs 3:11-12 where God himself disciplines those he loves, hinting that true wisdom welcomes the very rebuke a fool despises.

4:0.rendering: My son, pay attention to my wisdom, bend your ear to my understanding.
4:0.note: The Hebrew pairs "wisdom" (hokmah) with "understanding" (tevunah), echoing the same duo in Proverbs 1:2 and 2:2, as the father now turns from broad principles to the urgent, intimate danger of sexual folly.

4:1.rendering: so that you may guard discretion, and your lips may keep knowledge.
4:1.note: The Hebrew pairs "guard" with "lips" to echo how a city gatekeeper protects treasure, training the tongue as the first line of wisdom's defense.

4:2.rendering: The lips of a strange woman drip honey, and her palate is smoother than oil.
4:2.note: The Hebrew word for "strange" (zarah) hints at foreignness and otherness, painting the adulteress as an alluring outsider whose seductive words echo the dangerous wisdom of the forbidden tree.
