4:3.rendering: But in the end she is bitter as wormwood, sharp as a two-edged sword.
4:3.note: The Hebrew pairs "wormwood" (a nauseatingly bitter desert plant) with "two-edged sword," echoing the razor-sharp tongue of the adulteress from chapter 2.

4:4.rendering: Her feet go down to death, her steps take hold of Sheol.
4:4.note: The Hebrew word for "take hold" echoes the grasp of a trap or snare, painting her path as an active hunter that seizes the unwary into the realm of the dead.

4:5.rendering: She does not ponder the path of life, her steps wander without her knowing it.
4:5.note: The Hebrew word for "wander" echoes the same root used for the wayward wife herself, painting her life as one long, unconscious detour from the path of life.

4:6.rendering: So now, sons, listen to me. Do not turn aside from the words of my mouth.
4:6.note: The urgent call "listen to me" echoes the father's repeated pleas throughout chapters 1-9, forming a wisdom frame against the seductive path ahead.

4:7.rendering: Keep your path far from her, and do not come near the door of her house.
4:7.note: The Hebrew verb for "keep far" echoes the command to stay distant from idolatry in the Torah, turning sexual folly into a form of spiritual unfaithfulness.

8:8.rendering: Give to the wise and he will become wiser still, teach a righteous one and he will increase in learning.
8:8.note: The Hebrew word for "increase" here echoes the same root used for the growth of wisdom itself in chapter 1, showing that teaching the teachable sets off a chain reaction of multiplying insight.

8:9.rendering: The beginning of wisdom is the fear of the Lord, and the knowledge of the Holy One is insight.
8:9.note: This echoes Proverbs 1:7, bookending the opening collection with the same Hebrew word for "beginning" that can also mean "chief" or "best part."

8:10.rendering: For through me your days will be many, and years of life will be added to you.
8:10.note: Wisdom here echoes the tree of life in Proverbs 3:18, promising added years to those who embrace her.

8:11.rendering: If you are wise, you are wise for your own benefit, but if you scoff, you alone will bear it.
8:11.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "wise for yourself" echoes the tree of knowledge in Eden, where choosing wisdom or folly shapes your own life story.

8:12.rendering: Lady Folly is loud, she is gullible and knows nothing.
8:12.note: The Hebrew paints her as a noisy, seductive simpleton, the dark mirror image of Lady Wisdom calling from the same high places.

8:13.rendering: She sits at the doorway of her house, on a seat at the highest point of the city.
8:13.note: Wisdom built her house on the heights, but Folly perches at the same prominent spot, mimicking her rival.

8:14.rendering: To call out to those who pass by on the road, who are going straight on their way.
8:14.note: The Hebrew verb for "call out" echoes Wisdom's own urgent invitation in verse 3, but now it's Folly mimicking her voice from the highest point in the city.

8:15.rendering: "Whoever is naive, turn aside here," she says to him who lacks sense, "and stolen water is sweet, and bread eaten in secret is pleasant."
8:15.note: The seductive woman's invitation deliberately echoes Wisdom's exact words from verse 4, twisting the same call to the simple into an offer of hidden pleasures.

4:8.rendering: lest you give your splendor to others, and your years to one who is cruel,
4:8.note: The Hebrew for "splendor" (hod) is the same radiant word used for a king's majestic glory, warning that sexual folly can hand your hard-won dignity to strangers.

4:9.rendering: Strangers will feast on your strength, and your hard-earned wages will end up in a foreigner's house.
4:9.note: The verse's Hebrew word for "strangers" echoes the outsider who seduces in verses 3-6, showing how one unwise choice hands your life's produce to those outside your covenant community.

4:10.rendering: And at the end you will groan, when your flesh and your body are spent.
4:10.note: The Hebrew verb for "groan" is the same one used for the cries of the oppressed in Egypt, turning private regret into an exodus-like lament over self-inflicted ruin.

4:11.rendering: And you will say, "How I hated discipline, and my heart spurned correction!"
4:11.note: The Hebrew word for "hated" (sane) is the same strong verb used for God's hatred of evil, flipping the student's regret into a mirror of divine disgust at self-chosen ruin.

4:12.rendering: I did not listen to the voice of my teachers, and I did not incline my ear to my instructors.
4:12.note: The Hebrew word for "incline" echoes how wisdom herself cries out and calls us to bend toward her in the opening chapters.

4:13.rendering: I was almost in complete ruin, in the midst of the assembly and the congregation.
4:13.note: The Hebrew word for "almost" carries the sense of "a little more" or "in a moment," underscoring how close the fool came to total public disaster.

4:14.rendering: Drink water from your own cistern, and running water from your own well.
4:14.note: This vivid image draws on the ancient Near Eastern metaphor of a wife as a private, life-giving well (see Song 4:15), urging fidelity over the public promiscuity warned against in the chapter.

4:15.rendering: Should your springs overflow in the streets, your streams of water in the public squares?
4:15.note: The Hebrew word for "springs" (ma'yan) echoes the life-giving fountains of Eden and the Promised Land, now pictured as dangerously scattered instead of guarded at home.

9:0.rendering: A wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish son is the grief of his mother.
9:0.note: The Hebrew word for "grief" here is the same one used for bitter sorrow in childbirth, quietly echoing the pain a wayward child brings into the family.

9:1.rendering: Treasures gained by wickedness do not profit, but righteousness delivers from death.
9:1.note: The Hebrew word for "profit" echoes the name of Cain's descendant Tubal-Cain, the forger of bronze and iron tools, hinting that ill-gotten wealth, like his violent legacy, ultimately fails to save.

9:2.rendering: The Lord does not let the soul of the righteous go hungry, but he thrusts away the craving of the wicked.
9:2.note: This proverb echoes the manna story in Exodus, where God fed his people in the wilderness but let the greedy quail hoarders choke on their own excess.

9:3.rendering: A slack hand causes poverty, but the hand of the diligent makes rich.
9:3.note: The Hebrew word for "hand" (yad) repeats, contrasting the lazy fist that stays empty with the steady one that gathers wealth, a wisdom pattern echoed from Genesis 3's curse on toil.

9:4.rendering: A wise son gathers in summer, but a shameful son sleeps through harvest.
9:4.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "wise" (chakam) and "shameful" (mebush) turns farming into a mirror for character: diligence brings honor, laziness brings disgrace.

9:5.rendering: Blessings crown the head of the righteous, but violence covers the mouth of the wicked.
9:5.note: The Hebrew word for "covers" echoes the way bloodguilt or shame can overwhelm a guilty person, like a garment that smothers.

9:6.rendering: The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked will rot.
9:6.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew idea that a "name" (shem) carries enduring legacy, contrasting fragrant remembrance with the stench of decay.

9:7.rendering: The wise-hearted receives commands, but a chattering fool will come to ruin.
9:7.note: The Hebrew pairs "wise of heart" with the one who "takes" or "receives" instruction, contrasting the babbling fool who is literally "heavy of lip" and headed for a sudden fall.

8:16.rendering: "Stolen waters are sweet, and bread eaten in secret is delicious."
8:16.note: The Hebrew word for "sweet" (yimtaqu) echoes the seductive language of the adulteress in chapter 7, turning the imagery of illicit pleasure into a wisdom warning about deception.

8:17.rendering: But he does not know that the Rephaim are there, that her guests are in the depths of Sheol.
8:17.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "Rephaim" (shades of the dead) echoes the ghostly inhabitants of the underworld, turning this dinner party into a gateway to the grave.

4:16.rendering: Let them be for you alone, and not for strangers with you.
4:16.note: The Hebrew word for "alone" echoes the creation blessing of exclusive covenant partnership, turning private wells of intimacy into a picture of fidelity against the chapter's warnings of the loose woman.

4:17.rendering: Let your fountain be blessed, and rejoice in the wife of your youth.
4:17.note: The "fountain" echoes the garden imagery of Proverbs 5:15-16, portraying marital intimacy as a life-giving spring meant to be celebrated, not shared.

4:18.rendering: A lovely deer, a graceful doe: let her breasts satisfy you at all times, be captivated by her love always.
4:18.note: The Hebrew word for "captivated" pictures being led astray in an intoxicating way, turning the usual warning against wayward women into an invitation to be happily ensnared by your own wife's charms.

4:19.rendering: Why, my son, would you be infatuated with a forbidden woman, and embrace the bosom of a stranger?
4:19.note: The Hebrew word for "embrace" here echoes the intimate language of marital love in Song of Songs, exposing how we trade covenant joy for fleeting illusion.

4:20.rendering: For a man's ways are before the eyes of the LORD, and he watches all his paths.
4:20.note: The Hebrew verb for "watches" (p-l-s) pictures God actively leveling and smoothing a path, an image that echoes how wisdom herself makes straight the way for the righteous.

4:21.rendering: The wicked are captured by their own evil deeds, they are snared by the cords of their own sin.
4:21.note: The Hebrew word for "cords" echoes the binding ropes used to trap animals in ancient hunting, revealing how sin slowly tightens around us like a hunter's noose.

4:22.rendering: He will die for lack of discipline, and in the greatness of his folly he will stagger and be lost.
4:22.note: The Hebrew pairs "discipline" (musar) with "folly" (iwwelet) to show how rejecting one leads straight into the other, like a drunk losing his way.

9:8.rendering: Whoever walks in integrity walks securely, but whoever twists his paths will be found out.
9:8.note: The Hebrew word for "twists" (ʿiqqesh) echoes the same term used for Israel's crooked wilderness rebellion in Deuteronomy, showing how a life of detours inevitably gets exposed.

9:9.rendering: Whoever winks with the eye causes trouble, but a babbling fool will come to ruin.
9:9.note: The wink here is an ancient gesture of deceitful conspiracy, echoing the scheming eyes warned against in Proverbs 6:13.

9:10.rendering: The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.
9:10.note: In Hebrew the word for "fountain" echoes the life-giving springs of Eden and the temple, contrasting the wicked's hidden violence with words that truly sustain a community.

9:11.rendering: Hatred stirs up conflicts, but love covers over all offenses.
9:11.note: The Hebrew word for "covers" echoes the ritual language of atonement in Leviticus, hinting that everyday love does priestly work.

9:12.rendering: Wisdom resides on the lips of the discerning, but a rod is for the back of the one lacking sense.
9:12.note: The Hebrew pairs "wisdom on the lips" with "rod for the back," a stark ancient image of discipline that echoes Proverbs 13:24 and 22:15.

9:13.rendering: Wise people store up knowledge, but the mouth of a fool brings ruin near.
9:13.note: The Hebrew word for "store up" echoes how Israel stored manna in the wilderness, contrasting gathered wisdom with the fool's words that invite disaster.

9:14.rendering: A rich person's wealth is his fortified city, the poverty of the poor is their ruin.
9:14.note: This proverb uses the same Hebrew word for "ruin" as the flood that destroyed the earth in Genesis 6, hinting that poverty can feel like an overwhelming catastrophe.

9:15.rendering: The wage of the righteous leads to life, the produce of the wicked to sin.
9:15.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "produce" or "income," which elsewhere describes the harvest of the land, revealing how our character turns even our daily earnings toward either life or self-entangling wrongdoing.

3:0.rendering: Listen, sons, to a father's instruction, and pay attention so you can gain understanding.
3:0.note: The Hebrew word for "listen" is the same one used for the Shema, calling these sons to treat their father's words with the weight of covenant loyalty.

3:1.rendering: For I give you good teaching, do not abandon my instruction.
3:1.note: The Hebrew word for "teaching" here is torah, the same term used for God's own covenant instruction, linking a father's words to divine wisdom.

3:2.rendering: When I was a son to my father, tender and the only one in the sight of my mother,
3:2.note: The Hebrew word for "tender" echoes the fragile shoot in Isaiah 53:2, painting the young king as a vulnerable plant needing wisdom's protection.

3:3.rendering: He taught me and said to me, "Let your heart hold fast to my words. Keep my commands and live."
3:3.note: The Hebrew verb for "hold fast" echoes how Israel was to cling to the Lord himself in Deuteronomy, turning parental wisdom into covenant loyalty.

3:4.rendering: Get wisdom, get understanding, do not turn aside from the words of my mouth.
3:4.note: The double imperative "get, get" echoes the urgent cry of Wisdom personified in Proverbs 1 and 8, framing this as a father's urgent handover of the family treasure.

3:5.rendering: Do not forsake her, and she will guard you. Love her, and she will watch over you.
3:5.note: The Hebrew verbs for "guard" and "watch over" echo God's protective care in Psalm 121, personifying wisdom as a fierce guardian who returns the favor when loved.

3:6.rendering: The beginning of wisdom is: get wisdom. At the cost of all you have, get understanding.
3:6.note: This proverb's wordplay on "beginning" (reshit) echoes Genesis 1:1, urging wisdom as the first and most costly act of creation in our own lives.

3:7.rendering: Prize her highly and she will exalt you. She will honor you if you embrace her.
3:7.note: The Hebrew word for "prize highly" (salsheleha) plays on the idea of twisting or braiding her into your very being, like a cord that lifts you up.

2:0.rendering: My son, do not forget my teaching, but let your heart keep my commands.
2:0.note: The Hebrew pairs "forget" with "keep" in the heart, training the reader to internalize wisdom as the antidote to a wandering memory.

2:1.rendering: for they will add to you length of days, years of life, and wholeness.
2:1.note: The Hebrew word shalom, here rendered wholeness, echoes the Aaronic blessing and hints that wisdom's gifts go far beyond mere survival to a life of integrated peace.

2:2.rendering: Do not let kindness and truth abandon you. Bind them around your neck, write them on the tablet of your heart.
2:2.note: In the Hebrew these two words form a classic pair, hesed and emet, the same pair God uses to describe himself in Exodus 34:6.

2:3.rendering: Then you will find favor and good insight in the eyes of God and people.
2:3.note: The Hebrew pairs "favor" with "good insight" (sekel tov), an echo of the high priest's breastpiece that bore the names of God's people before both the Lord and the world.

2:4.rendering: Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding.
2:4.note: The Hebrew word for "lean" pictures a vine sagging on a trellis, an image of dependence that echoes the garden's original choice to trust self over God.

2:5.rendering: In all your ways know him intimately, and he will make your paths straight.
2:5.note: The Hebrew verb "know" (yada) here means more than information, it is the intimate, relational knowing of covenant love, echoed in God's promise to Israel.

2:6.rendering: Do not be wise in your own eyes. Fear the LORD and turn away from evil.
2:6.note: The Hebrew pairs "wise in your own eyes" with the command to "fear Yahweh," echoing the book's opening theme that true wisdom begins with awe of God, not self-reliance.

2:7.rendering: It will be healing to your flesh, and refreshment to your bones.
2:7.note: The Hebrew pairs "healing" (riph'ut) for your flesh with "drink" or "moistening" (shiqquy) for your bones, picturing wisdom as medicine that restores from the inside out.

9:16.rendering: Whoever heeds instruction is on the path to life, but whoever ignores correction wanders astray.
9:16.note: The Hebrew word for "path" echoes the wisdom journey motif from Proverbs 1-9, where listening or refusing to listen determines whether you stay on the road of life or veer into ruin.

9:17.rendering: Whoever conceals hatred has lying lips, and whoever spreads slander is a fool.
9:17.note: The Hebrew pairs the two lines with a surprising twist: the one who hides his hate ends up speaking lies, while the open slanderer is simply revealed as a fool, exposing how both paths distort community.

9:18.rendering: When words are many, transgression does not cease, but the one who restrains his lips is prudent.
9:18.note: The Hebrew pairs the “multitude of words” with inevitable wrongdoing, a wisdom motif echoed from Ecclesiastes 5:3 and James 3:2.

9:19.rendering: The tongue of the righteous is choice silver, but the heart of the wicked is worth little.
9:19.note: In Hebrew, "tongue" and "heart" form a subtle parallel, contrasting what a person says with who they truly are.

9:20.rendering: The lips of the righteous feed many, but fools die for lack of sense.
9:20.note: In Hebrew, "feed" plays on the image of shepherding a flock, contrasting wise words that sustain a community with the self-destructive isolation of the senseless.

9:21.rendering: The blessing of the LORD makes rich, and he adds no sorrow with it.
9:21.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew root for "toil" (etsev), contrasting God's effortless blessing with the anxious labor that often brings grief.

9:22.rendering: Folly delights a fool like a prank, but a person of understanding finds joy in wisdom.
9:22.note: The Hebrew word for "delight" here is the same one used for God's pleasure in creation, flipping the script on what truly satisfies.

9:23.rendering: What the wicked dreads will overtake him, but the desire of the righteous will be granted.
9:23.note: The Hebrew word for "dread" (məgôrat) echoes the terror of judgment in earlier wisdom texts, flipping the script so fear itself becomes the hunter.

3:8.rendering: She will give to your head a garland of grace, and she will crown you with a beautiful diadem.
3:8.note: In Hebrew, the word for "garland" echoes the leafy wreaths awarded to victors in ancient contests, a surprising image of wisdom crowning the humble seeker like a champion.

3:9.rendering: Listen, my son, and accept my words, so the years of your life will be many.
3:9.note: The Hebrew pairs "listen" and "accept" with the promise of "many years," echoing the father-son wisdom tradition that links obedience to long life, as in Deuteronomy 6:2.

3:10.rendering: I have taught you the way of wisdom, I have led you in straight paths.
3:10.note: The Hebrew verb for "led" echoes God's guidance of Israel through the wilderness, turning parental instruction into a new exodus.

3:11.rendering: When you walk, your steps will not be cramped, and when you run, you will not stumble.
3:11.note: The Hebrew word for "stumble" echoes the same root used for the fool's collapse in Proverbs 24:16, contrasting the wise person's steady path with the wicked's repeated falls.

3:12.rendering: Hold on to instruction, do not let it go. Guard it, for it is your life.
3:12.note: The Hebrew word for "hold on" echoes the gripping action of a wrestler refusing to release his opponent, turning wisdom into a fierce, life-or-death embrace.

3:13.rendering: Do not enter the path of the wicked, and do not walk in the way of evil men.
3:13.note: The Hebrew verb for "enter" echoes the very first verse of the book, where the blessed person refuses to walk, stand, or sit with the wicked.

3:14.rendering: Avoid it, do not pass by it, turn away from it and pass on.
3:14.note: The Hebrew piles up five urgent verbs in one verse, like a frantic detour sign hammered into the road of wisdom.

3:15.rendering: For they cannot sleep unless they have done wrong, they are robbed of sleep unless they make someone stumble.
3:15.note: The Hebrew word for “stumble” (kashal) echoes the earlier warning in this chapter about the path of the wicked becoming like darkness where people fall, turning their insomnia into a twisted meditation on evil.

2:8.rendering: Honor the Lord with your wealth, and with the firstfruits of all your produce.
2:8.note: This echoes the ancient Israelite practice of offering the very first and best of the harvest at the sanctuary, a tangible way of acknowledging that everything comes from God before claiming any for yourself.

2:9.rendering: Then your barns will be filled with plenty, and your vats will burst open with new wine.
2:9.note: This echoes Deuteronomy 28's covenant blessings, where overflowing storehouses picture the tangible fruit of walking in wisdom's fear of the Lord.

2:10.rendering: My son, do not reject the Lord's instruction, and do not loathe his rebuke.
2:10.note: The Hebrew word for "rebuke" here echoes the same root used for God's fatherly discipline of Israel in Deuteronomy 8:5, painting correction as a loving craftsman's tool rather than random pain.

2:11.rendering: For Yahweh disciplines the one he loves, just as a father disciplines the son in whom he delights.
2:11.note: The Hebrew word for "disciplines" (yokhach) also means "to prove or test," echoing a father's loving refinement like a metalsmith purifying silver.

2:12.rendering: Blessed is the one who finds wisdom, and the one who gets understanding.
2:12.note: The Hebrew word for "blessed" here is the same as in Psalm 1, painting the wise person as a deeply rooted, flourishing tree by the water.

2:13.rendering: For her profit is better than profit from silver, and her revenue than gold.
2:13.note: The Hebrew word for "profit" (sakhar) echoes the name of the merchant patriarch Issachar, whose tribe became a trading people, turning wisdom's value into a vivid marketplace image.

2:14.rendering: She is more precious than jewels, and nothing you desire compares with her.
2:14.note: The Hebrew word for "jewels" (peninim) likely means coral or pearls, ancient symbols of hard-won underwater treasure that echoes wisdom's elusive, high-cost value.

2:15.rendering: In her right hand is length of days, in her left hand are riches and honor.
2:15.note: The Hebrew word for "length of days" echoes the tree of life in Genesis 3:22 and Proverbs 3:18, portraying Wisdom as the path back to Eden's blessing.

3:16.rendering: For they eat the bread of wickedness, and they drink the wine of violence.
3:16.note: The Hebrew wordplay pairs "bread" and "wine" with "wickedness" and "violence," twisting the language of ordinary meals into a portrait of how the wicked consume evil as daily sustenance.

3:17.rendering: But the path of the righteous is like the light of dawn, that grows brighter and brighter until full day.
3:17.note: This Hebrew word for "path" echoes the "way" of wisdom from chapter 1, painting a sunrise that steadily banishes darkness.

3:18.rendering: The way of the wicked is like deep darkness. They do not know what makes them stumble.
3:18.note: The Hebrew word for "stumble" echoes the same root used for the blind groping in Isaiah 59:10, painting the wicked as self-blinded wanderers.

3:19.rendering: My son, pay attention to my words, incline your ear to my sayings.
3:19.note: The Hebrew pairs "pay attention" (qashab) with "incline your ear" like a shepherd training a lamb to tune its hearing to the shepherd's voice alone.

3:20.rendering: Let them not depart from your eyes, guard them within your heart.
3:20.note: The Hebrew verb for "guard" echoes the cherubim stationed to guard the tree of life in Eden, turning wisdom into a matter of protecting the source of true life.

3:21.rendering: For they are life to those who find them, and healing to all their flesh.
3:21.note: The Hebrew pairs "life" (chayyim) with bodily "healing" (marpe), echoing the tree of life in Eden and the later promise that the leaves of the new-creation tree will heal the nations.

3:22.rendering: Guard your heart with all vigilance, for from it flow the springs of life.
3:22.note: The Hebrew word for "guard" echoes the cherubim stationed to protect Eden's tree of life, hinting this proverb trains us to reclaim what was lost.

3:23.rendering: Put away from you crooked speech, and put perverse lips far from you.
3:23.note: The Hebrew pairs "crooked" (iqqesh) with "perverse" (lahaz), echoing the twisted paths warned against in the very next verse.

9:24.rendering: When the storm sweeps past, the wicked are no more, but the righteous stand firm forever.
9:24.note: The Hebrew word for "stand firm" (yesod) echoes the "foundation" a wise person builds on rock in the Sermon on the Mount, turning this proverb into a meditation on lasting stability.

9:25.rendering: Like vinegar to the teeth and smoke to the eyes, so is the sluggard to those who send him.
9:25.note: The Hebrew wordplay links the lazy messenger to irritating substances that linger and sting, revealing how unreliability wounds trust like physical pain.

9:26.rendering: The fear of the LORD adds days to life, but the years of the wicked will be cut short.
9:26.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "fear" (yir'at) echoing the opening of the book in 1:7, rooting long life not in luck but in reverent awe before God.

9:27.rendering: The hope of the righteous is joy, but the expectation of the wicked will perish.
9:27.note: The Hebrew pairs "hope" (literally "what is waited for") with joy for the righteous, while the wicked's "expectation" dissolves into nothing, an early echo of Psalm 1's two diverging paths.

9:28.rendering: The way of the Lord is a stronghold for the blameless, but it is terror for those who do evil.
9:28.note: The Hebrew word for "stronghold" also means "refuge," echoing how the Lord himself shelters the upright while exposing the wicked, a theme that runs from the Psalms into the prophets.

9:29.rendering: The righteous will never be shaken, but the wicked will not dwell in the land.
9:29.note: This echoes the wisdom psalms' promise that the righteous are like trees planted by streams, their roots too deep for removal.

9:30.rendering: The mouth of the righteous produces wisdom, but the tongue of the perverse will be cut off.
9:30.note: The Hebrew pairs "fruit" (yafriach) for the righteous mouth with sudden "cutting off" for the crooked tongue, evoking a tree that bears good fruit versus one violently severed.

9:31.rendering: The lips of the righteous know what is acceptable, but the mouth of the wicked is twisted.
9:31.note: The Hebrew word for "twisted" here echoes the same term for perverted justice in the law codes, revealing how speech can either align with or distort God's created order.

2:16.rendering: Her ways are ways of pleasantness, and all her paths are peace.
2:16.note: The Hebrew word for "pleasantness" (no'am) echoes the delight of God's own presence, turning wisdom's road into a garden of shalom.

2:17.rendering: She is a tree of life to those who take hold of her, and those who hold her tight are called blessed.
2:17.note: This echoes the garden's tree of life, now personified as wisdom, inviting us back to fruitful flourishing through patient embrace.

2:18.rendering: Yahweh founded the earth by wisdom, he established the heavens by understanding.
2:18.note: This echoes the ancient Near Eastern idea of creation by divine skill, but grounds it in Yahweh's personal wisdom, a theme that later blooms in John's prologue about the Word.

2:19.rendering: By his knowledge the deeps were split open, and the clouds drop down the dew.
2:19.note: The same Hebrew word for "knowledge" that opens Proverbs (1:7) now unlocks creation itself, splitting the primordial waters like in Genesis 1.

2:20.rendering: My son, do not let these slip from your eyes: guard sound wisdom and discretion.
2:20.note: The Hebrew verb for "guard" echoes the cherubim stationed to protect the tree of life in Eden, inviting us to actively treasure wisdom as the path back to human flourishing.

2:21.rendering: They will be life for your soul, and grace for your neck.
2:21.note: The Hebrew pairs "life for your soul" with "grace for your neck," echoing the earlier command to bind wisdom like a necklace (v.3,21) so that character visibly adorns the whole person.

2:22.rendering: Then you will walk on your way securely, and your foot will not stumble.
2:22.note: The Hebrew verb for "stumble" echoes the same root used when the serpent "bruised" the heel in Genesis 3:15, hinting at restored safe footing under wisdom's guard.

2:23.rendering: When you lie down, you will not be afraid. You will lie down and your sleep will be sweet.
2:23.note: The Hebrew verb for "lie down" echoes the same word used for the peaceful rest of the land in Leviticus 26:6, painting wisdom as the path to covenant rest.

3:24.rendering: Let your eyes look straight ahead, and your gaze be fixed right in front of you.
3:24.note: This command uses the Hebrew idiom for moral focus, echoing the "way" imagery that frames the whole chapter like a father tracing a safe path through wilderness.

3:25.rendering: Ponder the path of your feet, then all your ways will be established.
3:25.note: The Hebrew word for "ponder" means to make level or straight, echoing the wisdom path that straightens what sin has twisted.

3:26.rendering: Do not turn aside to the right or to the left; keep your foot from evil.
3:26.note: The command to avoid swerving right or left echoes the king's road in Deuteronomy 5:32 and 17:20, training the heart in unwavering focus.

2:24.rendering: Do not be afraid of sudden terror, or of the storm that comes upon the wicked.
2:24.note: The Hebrew word for "terror" here echoes the "dread" that fell on Egypt during the plagues, reminding Israel that chaos strikes the guilty, not those who walk in wisdom.

2:25.rendering: For the Lord will be your confidence, and he will keep your foot from being caught.
2:25.note: The Hebrew for "confidence" (kesel) usually means "folly" elsewhere in Proverbs, so this verse playfully reclaims the word to say God himself becomes the wise person's true security.

2:26.rendering: Do not withhold good from those who deserve it, when it is in your power to act.
2:26.note: The Hebrew literally says "do not withhold good from its owners," a vivid image of goods that already belong to the needy.

2:27.rendering: Do not say to your neighbor, "Go, and come back, and tomorrow I will give it," when you have it with you right now.
2:27.note: The Hebrew word for "neighbor" (rea) echoes the command to love your neighbor as yourself, turning a practical warning against stingy delay into a test of covenant love.

2:28.rendering: Do not plot harm against your neighbor, who lives trustingly beside you.
2:28.note: The Hebrew verb for "plot harm" echoes the same word used for Joseph's brothers scheming against him in Genesis, turning a domestic warning into a subtle reminder that betrayal begins in the heart long before it reaches the hands.

2:29.rendering: Do not contend with a person for no reason, when he has done you no harm.
2:29.note: The Hebrew verb here for "contend" carries legal overtones of a lawsuit, warning against stirring up needless conflict in the community.

2:30.rendering: Do not envy the violent man, or choose any of his ways.
2:30.note: This warning against envying the oppressor draws on the ancient Near Eastern idea that the violent man's path looks like the road to success, yet Proverbs insists it leads to ruin while wisdom's path brings life.

2:31.rendering: For the crooked is an abomination to the Lord, but the upright are in his close confidence.
2:31.note: The word "abomination" echoes the same strong disgust God shows toward idolatry, flipping the expected target from ritual to relational betrayal.

2:32.rendering: The curse of the LORD is on the house of the wicked, but he blesses the home of the righteous.
2:32.note: The Hebrew word for "home" here echoes the tabernacle's dwelling place, hinting that righteousness invites God's presence while wickedness drives it away.

2:33.rendering: He mocks the mockers, but to the humble he gives grace.
2:33.note: The Hebrew word for "mock" (lits) creates a sharp wordplay with "grace" (chen), showing how God flips the proud person's script.

2:34.rendering: The wise will inherit honor, but fools get disgrace as their promotion.
2:34.note: The Hebrew word for "promotion" is the same one used for Israel's elevation out of Egypt, turning the fool's supposed rise into public shame.
